This functions as an immunity against the genuine question of its own historical genesis.

A concrete example of this problem was brought to my attention, firstly by me. I was struggling with another problem I dubbed, the “Socrates and Buddha” problem, wherein I was trying to reconcile something in Hegel but found the solution in Steiner. What I was trying to understand was why specific individuals become world-historical vessels. For example, why Socrates, and why Buddha? On one level, their historical emergence seems almost contingent, depending on fragile transmissions from particular disciples. Yet their archetypal roles are necessary: Socrates embodies rational self-examination, looking outward, and Buddha, universal compassion and inner liberation. Inner and outer. The Hegelian answer is once again to say that the necessity works through contingency. Abstractly, and logically, this is the case. The essential truth of their teachings prevails despite historical accidents. But this remains a logical abstraction, a retrospective justification, not an explanation. Hegel’s grand “cunning of reason” is essentially a logical placeholder. Found within Steiner is an elaboration of the how turning into the metaphysics of Spirit’s agency. What Hegel calls World-Spirit, Steiner describes as spiritual hierarchies working through karmic agreements, and pre-earthly soul choices. The individual, in this case Socrates, Buddha, or a disciple, is not a passive node but a free collaborator. The soul chooses its path and conditions before incarnation; with inspiration requiring a receptive human vessel. Even the possessing of Spirit by man demands the individual’s consent. Steiner preserves Hegel’s insight, that necessity works through contingency, while grounding it in concrete processes and freedom. Suffering is not flattened into a logical node. The world we live in hears cries of anguish to God, the heavens, or anyone listening and receives this suffering as a substantive event, not as a mere contingency. Pain, pleasure, love, regret, insight, “eureka” moments, and more, are all felt by the whole. By subordinating all contingency into logical necessity, the Hegelian response collapses the living process of spiritual creation into a pre-determined script. In Steiner’s terms, it replaces the creative, sometimes improvisational, work of spiritual beings (the hierarchies) with an impersonal, logical automation. The “why” of Socrates or Hegel is reduced to a nodal point in a syllogism, not understood as the result of concrete spiritual guidance, karmic trajectories, and free spiritual agency working through historical contingencies. The Hegelian response thus embodies the very “flatness” of Spirit my initial intuition-turned-critique identifies.

The second time this was brought to my intention was when a friend introduced an alternative viewpoint: what if the History of Philosophy is actually the capstone of Hegel’s project? In hearing his explanation, it made sense, and I quickly and intuitively responded with the fact that this sounds like something Steiner’s project hopes to aim. After a day of thinking and talking about it, I agreed with him. He proposed that the History of Philosophy is the true capstone of the system, not just in subject, but in its reality since it “describes and enacts”. History, in this view, is the singular “description-enaction” where thought, and it’s object become one. Initially, this struck me as profound, and akin to what Steiner’s spiritual science aims at. This being a living unity of knowledge and reality. After a day, I disagreed once more, recognizing a subtle but decisive limitation. Even in this reading, the practical summons that emerges from Hegel’s history (the call to enact freedom consciously) remains a logical consequence of achieved Absolute Knowledge. It’s still, ultimately, a movement within philosophical comprehension. Thus, while the system can culminate in a transformative insight that bridges theory and practice, this transition itself is still comprehended and derived from the standpoint of completed philosophy. The “capstone” is therefore not an open, creative entry into history, but the final consummate description that logically necessitates a new form of practice. This is what preserves Hegel’s circle; that the end of theoretical history yields a practical imperative, yet both remain moments within the self-enclosed speculation of the Idea. The problem of contingency remains, although it might be a bit misinformed to call it a problem, since it’s more the lack of something the system can provide. And to fair, Hegel’s project is expressly aimed in solving philosophy, nothing more. The naming of it as a problem is moreso a politic move in order to urge Hegelians to see beyond, within the narrative of my own journey. Besides, even if the problem of contingency were resolved, a more fundamental limitation remains.

The Problem of Qualitative Immediacy

If Hegel’s system stumbles on historical contingency, then it faces a bigger problem when confronted with the raw being of the world, and the unmediated quality of living perception, which is something the logic cannot produce or capture. I will say it again: the logical method CANNOT generate or account for the qualitative immediacy, the raw, sensuous, being, of living experience. The system must treat the shock of color, the pang of grief, or the awe before a work like Moby-Dick, as mere logical predicates, moments in the self-determination of the Concept. Yet this formalism reaches its crisis not with sense-perception at the “bottom”, but with the very peak of spiritual concreteness: the encounter with a person. For Hegel, all individuality is conceptually mediated. A person is an individuality in principle. The system provides room for Christ as the necessary conceptual principle of God-man unity, as well as the Individual Individuality found within the structure of Universal, Particular, and Individual. Unfortunately, this Christ-principle is not the sensuous living person encountered in prayer or revelation. The immediacy of a who is always already reduced to a logically mediated what. The system may be able to generate the Idea of Christ, but it cannot host the encounter. Thus, in its treatment of its own most supremely concrete individual, the system points implicitly to a domain beyond its formal grasp: the domain of non-Conceptually mediated personal being. This is not a failure of Hegel’s Christianity as much as it is a necessary result of the formalism of the Concept. If logic can’t hold the living Christ, what is it to hold? Only his shadow. The formalism described here in full clarity is that since all objects are apprehended in and through their logical relation as necessary, universal, and mediated by Concept, Concept’s organization and giving intelligibility to content (hence shaping the object) is a formalism. Concept only encounters objects as structured and generated by itself. This is what we might call the formalism of the Concept. Everything must be translated into the currency of logical relations. As a result, the system is structurally blind to the very datum of consciousness, which is the very real, almost startling, given presence of sensation and feeling. This is a point-blank categorical exclusion demanded by Hegel’s sovereign, generative thinking. The map is drawn entirely in the ink of mediation; it has no symbols for the immediate, being of reality. Therefore, it can describe the logical necessity of sense-certainty as a failed form of consciousness, but it can’t originate the sensation itself. This explanatory deficit reveals that thinking’s sovereignty is bought at the price of excluding the most intimate layer of being. The problems of contingency and quality converge to reveal the final lack: life.

Note: The sophisticated Hegelian here might offer a rebuttal, “Life isn’t missing from the system! It appears explicitly in the Science of Logic as a deduced category of the Idea, the moment where the Concept begins to grasp itself as a self-organizing totality”. For Hegel, this logical category is the truth and essence of life. However, this very defense proves the critique. Hegel provides the concept of life, its logical schema, but not the living reality itself. This is the ultimate expression of the map-territory divide.

The Convergent Failure of Both Problems

In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God. The same was in the beginning with God. All things were made by him.

Let us re-establish what Hegel’s project is, in light of this. It is the exposition of the Word, that flawless logical blueprint, the Logos as the complete ratio and relational grammar of the universe. After all, the entire Science of Logic can be read as an onto-logical proof of God. This is the God before creation. Hegel’s system is this divine logic, self-contained, and self-justifying in all its grandeur. Given this, Goethe’s final lines in Faust Part II, Chorus Mysticus6, points to what the logical Word cannot accomplish on its own:


All that is transient
Is but a parable;
What is inadequate,
Here becomes an event;
The indescribable
Here is accomplished;
The eternal feminine
Pulls us upwards
—Goethe, “Chorus Mysticus”, Faust Part 2.

These lines conclude the entire Faust drama, spoken by a mystical chorus. Hegel, the philosopher, sees the world of becoming. He sees birth, growth, decay, death, and he correctly interprets it. He sees that these fleeting moments that are coming to be and ceasing to be are not the ultimate reality, they point to a higher ideality. All that is transient, is but a parable indeed, one that Hegel has deciphered, showing how every finite thing is a moment in the self-unfolding of the infinite Idea. In this, Goethe and Hegel are indeed fellow travelers. However, the inadequate becoming an event is a stage which philosophical logic cannot achieve. It is the moment of realization and incarnation, where Hegel by his own standard, or method, must cease. Because he must cease here, the “event to be done” is Spirit’s historical unfolding, grasped retrospectively by philosophy. Above all, as the famous saying goes, the owl of Minerva spreads its wings only with the falling of dusk. For Goethe, the conceptual blueprint, no matter how magnificent, is inadequate. It lacks the power to become an “event to be done”. Hegel’s system is not wrong about life per se, it simply offers life as a specter. It is a chronicle of past reconciliations written in the dried out ink of logic, as opposed to an encounter with the present, living creative act. Its very completeness is its sterility, and what results in what we identify as the fetishism at the heart of the system. Hegel sought Spirit in thought and found it there. His intention was quite noble and very profound. The intention was to transcend the human subject in thought and align its consciousness with the objective, creative World-Spirit. Yet, in an ironic reversal, he did not use logic as a medium to connect with living Spirit, instead he surrendered himself to the logical Idea, making the completed system (Absolute Idea) the ultimate object of his devotion. As Steiner argues, he committed a “fetishism of logic”. He correctly discovered Spirit within human thinking, but then made the categorical error of believing “that this spirit of human thinking, when developed to its highest power, is the World-Spirit” (GA 108)7. Thus, he surrendered to the pinnacle of ideation, not to the ideal creative source that produces the very capacity for ideation. The living generative reality of Spirit was replaced in his system by the worship of its own flawless shadow. The tool is the idol. Hence, the system becomes a monument to its own methodological triumph, a self-enclosed sanctuary where Logic is the supreme activity of God. That living, suffering, sacrificing, creative reality of Spirit, the very same Spirit Hegel claimed to unveil, retreats into the inaccessible beyond, visible only as a silhouette cast upon walls. The philosopher, being left in a temple of sublime shadows, mistakes the play of forms for the presence of divinity. Hegel’s philosophy articulates the perfect, living plan of the world, but it still remains a plan. It is the Word that has not yet spoken itself into flesh, the ratio that has not yet suffered the risk of incarnation. This ultimately is the chasm between the map and the territory. Hegel gives us Logos in logical purity.

The Ambassadors (1533), Hans Holbein the Younger
Holbein’s The Ambassadors represents a world of perfectly mastered symbols (notice the tools of measurement). Yet at its feet lies a distorted skull, or the undenaible reality of death: an unassimilable contigency, and the immediacy of memento mori. Death may be the most intense, and of course most easily accessible, non-negotiable instance of qualitative immediacy. Every person needs to contend with their own mortality. Even so, it can only be seen truly by shifting one’s perspective away from the brilliant surface of the map. So too must we view Hegel’s logical construction to grasp the living Spirit it occludes. Hegel famously critiqued the phrase “Spirit is a bone” in the Phenomenology of Spirit as the error of misplaced concreteness, the dead end of finding Spirit in the world. Ironically, he did not escape crafting it’s logical twin: “Idea [here meant as generated reality] is a syllogism". It’s a great irony that this skull represents that very same critique as self-applied.

The consequence is a constitutive, ethical-aesthetic blindness. Because the system cannot host a genuine encounter, it fails before the very realities it seeks to spiritualize. It can rationally reconstruct Melville’s posthumous recognition, but it cannot feel the weight of his obscurity or recognize the divine inspiration (and trust me, Moby Dick was divinely inspired) as a living current. The tragic particularity of suffering and the surplus of meaning in genius become logical nodes in a syllogism, never events to be lived. I think it is fair to call this a limitation, at this point. The limitation reveals its own imperative. If the perfect blueprint feels lifeless, and if the flawless logic cannot account for its own genesis in history, or its failure before a crying heart, then the task isn’t to refine the blueprint. It’s to seek a builder, or in clearer terms, shift from the kind of reflective contemplation philosophy provides, to developing a more active capacity to eventually participate in the living activity that enacts philosophy. That living activity of Spirit which labors, sacrifices, and loves that blueprint into existence is what we seek. Fundamentally, this is what I think is clarified by the Steinerian turn. From the closed circle of thought thinking itself, to the open development of capacities that allows consciousness to interface with the beings who think the world into being. This marks the passage from the philosophy of the Concept, to the science of the Spirit that utters it.

Steinerian turn…

The “Steinerian turn”, so to say, is deeply informed by Goethe’s revolutionary method, which exchanges the spectator stance or pseudo-Archimedean point of thinkers like Darwin, for an active, imaginative “slipping into” organisms, or beings. Where Hegel’s retrospective method can only logically reconstruct the concept of life, Goethe’s practice of inwardly tracing the metamorphic gestures of a plant in Metamorphosis of Plants8, and so experiencing a formal law of expansion and contraction, begins to reunite the knower with a generative force (the generative force of that particular being), which is in actuality the generative force. Steiner radicalized this into a conscious spiritual science, where through disciplined inner activity, the observer transcends representational thinking to experience, not just in deducing, the spiritual reality actively shaping the phenomenal world. There is much to be said about the moment of recursion that Steiner pioneers in Goethe’s approach. Nevertheless, for now, I will refer only back to the section on the importance of recursion in a system of totality. Steiner’s continuation of Goethe here fulfills the imperative to move from Hegel’s perfect “map” as Concept, to the living territory of creative Spirit.

There is an unacknowledged reader tagging along this blog post: the committed Hegelian. After me “totally obliterating Hegel” and proving he’s crazy and nonsensical and such, I would like to preview a few responses I’ve received from Hegelians, and where they go wrong. The dynamic between us, as interlocutors, is defined by a conflict of totalities. The “Hegelian”, let’s say, is claiming totality, just as the “Steinerian” (or any person whose system accurately captures the “beyond” of Hegel’s system) is. The situation is quite similar to the very beginning of this article, where claims to the totality of the object are made. Practically we can demonstrate the result: when confronted with a being, a spiritual being, such as an Angel, the Hegelian will interpret it as a Vorstellung (representation) that must be subordinated by the Concept. From this vantage, the Steinerian appears to be stuck in picture-thinking, mistaking an immediate mystical representation for true ideality, which for Hegel resides solely in the mediated movement of the Concept itself. After all, the true mysticism, claims Hegel, is in speculative thinking. Mysticism for him is a product of the Understanding. This isn’t a dismissal of the Steinerian, if I were to steel man this position. It is a correction, it is counsel to go further and meet the Concept. This is a profound reduction of the Steinerian standpoint. When the Steinerian says the Angel is Idea, the Idea is meant in the same sense as Hegel, but as something more concrete than Hegel’s Conceptual Idea. It refers to active, intelligent beings who are architects of spiritual realities and can be perceived beyond ordinary sense-experience. The conflict is surely amplified by a shared vocabulary that masks divergent realities. Here is another example: When Beinsa Douno writes that he spoke to the Slavic folk-spirit, the Hegelian comprehends this as a conceptual grasp of historical and logical necessity, such that a concrete figure (man) represents the soul of the Slavic people. The Steinerian says: no, he talked with an actual person, a spiritual person. He engaged in a participatory acquaintance with a conscious, guiding intelligence that was not a human. One of these standpoints is a knowledge about, reducing the thing to a representative of the thing (which is quite Kantian), and the other is knowledge of the thing-in-itself directly, by acquaintance. The highest practical example (that is to say, an example in practice) can be found by reading what Hegel says about the plant and what is logically entailed by it. Once this is finished, the Metamorphosis of Plants by Goethe provides a stunning parallel (although it is not entirely correspondent to Steiner’s project, it is in the same Spirit of what a project of post-Hegelian totality looks like). In one text, a kind of categorization is going on, and in the other, the subject is fully participating in the being of the object.

An issue being faced thus far is on language. Shared languages creates an illusion of common object, suggesting either a need for proprietary terms to dispel this conflation, or perhaps the strategic use of Hegel’s own language to reveal where his system points faithfully beyond itself. As of now, I am currently leaning towards the latter. This impasse resembles the reactions to the Ontological Argument. One either grasps it as a self-evident truism, or misunderstands and places that misunderstanding on part of the OA itself. The limit is not logical, but of the possibility of the subject’s being itself, and so it suggests to me that the ultimate resolution might not be argumentative, but transformative. Requiring a direct spiritual encounter- be it with an angel or Christ or by being instigated by psychadelics- that irrevocably shatters Hegelian Orthodoxy in a way that it cannot conceptually absorb. When put that way, it sounds almost threatening, that the solution is forced initiation, because that is what initiation is, a staged or controlled revelation.

The map is complete, with the relation between the map and the territory being that the map is the territory’s logically concluded past. A new domain is necessary to enter this territory as an ongoing creative future. Anthroposophy (Steiner’s system) seeks to enter this territory with the subject as an active collaborator within the Absolute. In light of this, allow me to borrow a metaphor from the earliest part of this article. The great anatomist of frameworks, Northrop Frye, sought a method for immanent genesis, one derived from the total structure of literature, and achieved it. By the same token, Hegel achieved this reality for itself, having sought an immanent genesis of the totality of totalities. Truly, there is no better comparison. Hegel himself is the ultimate critic, having given an immanent criticism of all there can be criticized. His system is the complete and perfect review of the “cosmic text”. Consider a work of immense complexity. A tapestry of history and references. The masterful critic can unveil its architecture. Perhaps he or she can explain how this or that detail relates to the whole, or how the prose supports the ethos of such and such part. The critical act is one of profound recovery and understanding. Yet for all its brilliance, the hermeneutic remains retrospective. The critic can reconstruct the logic of the fall of man in the Bible, but the critic did not cause the plummet. The critic can explain the symbol, but is not enduring the lived reality from which it exists, such that it is conveyed in the form firstly encountered in the text. The genius of criticism is to comprehend a world already made, not in making the world anew, nor in creation in general. Steiner’s project, therefore, is not to be a better critic, but to become a potential author, and train you to become one as well. The change lies in the move from perfecting interpretation to developing the faculties to participate in the “writing” (as a matter of metaphor, of course). Enough has been done to analyze the divine logic, but not enough has been done to collaborate with the divine author.

In laying out the nature of truth, being, and the general arc of my narrative, there are bound to be accusations levied at me from a Hegelian flavoring. The first is a common one. It asserts that Steiner’s quest for real-time, participatory knowledge fails to grasp that retrospective comprehension is not a philosophical limitation, but the very form in which Truth actualizes itself, and that what he calls a “higher” activity is already the immanent, self-unfolding work of Concept. This is correct, however what is not grasped is that anthroposophy, as explained above, addresses a different activity altogether. Steiner fully acknowledges that retrospection is the mode of philosophical comprehension. His critique is that this mode is passive in relation to the creative act. The Concept’s “self-unfolding work” in this case is logically reconstructed after the fact by the philosopher. There is a necessary addition found in the capacity to co-participate in the unfolding as it occurs, such that Concept is not actually constitutive of that self-unfolding work. In other words, retrospective comprehension is the form of Truth-as-Known, but anthroposophy aims at Truth-as-Lived-Creation. The former is certainly necessary, but insufficient for the consciousness that seeks not just to understand Spirit, but to work with and within it.

Sidenote: It is almost inevitable that the philosophically minded (meant as an insult) will hear the words “reflection” or “reflective act” and construct a straw man to attack as if I am operating with the premature logic of Essence that Hegel dispenses with by the time the system develops to the Concept. The individual capable of laying out the whole path of Hegel’s philosophy; one capable of understanding the distinction between Essence and Concept, is surely someone with enough expertise to not make such a blunder in thought. Certainly I am no expert, but absolutely not a novice. It reminds me of that one tweet that goes, “Little Caesars tastes so good when you don’t have a b—- in your ear telling you it’s nasty”, only it’s to the tune of “Talking with people feels so good when you don’t have people intentionally misinterpreting you”.

Another accusation is in the same form as the previous, that the thinker is somehow misguided and not accounting for how the Concept already subsumes what one is saying. “Is Steiner not simply mistaking the acquisition of new perceptual content (or spiritual data) for the development of an entirely new faculty, when from a Hegelian standpoint any new immediacy is merely fresh material for the one sovereign faculty of thinking and Reason to mediate and comprehend”? This may sound devastating, and it is. For the Hegelian. For the Steinerian, the claim that “any new material is fresh material for thinking” proves his or her point! It confines all experience within the form of reflective thought. Steiner insists that spiritual perception is not just new content, but a qualitatively different relationship to reality, where the perceiver does not stand over and against an object, but consciously lives within the being of that object. The faculty is new because it overcomes the subject-object distinction inherent in sense-perception and logical reflection (and if the position is held that Hegel also overcomes the subject-object divide in philosophy, then at least it overcomes it in a way previously not standardized before). It is a participatory knowing, not an observational thinking. To call this “new material” is to reduce an active to a passive.

One final accusation is a rather dogmatic retort. It comes in the form of the assertion that in the encounter of spiritual reality, take Christ for example, the Hegelian will assert that the reality experienced of a Christ encounter, is entirely negotiable with the Concept, or Hegelian Idea. This groundless response is the last refuge for someone who truly does not understand what is at stake. Not only was this position dealt with in the “The Problem of Qualitative Immediacy”, it is not honest. The full, honest, position is that super-sensible reality, such as Christ encounters, are either mistaken contingencies or merely the raw material for the Concept’s self-unfolding. Imagine telling someone who has had a Christ encounter that they did not meet a person, but a logical predicate in the dialectic of the Absolute. They would rightly respond: “I didn’t experience a ‘Concept’, I experienced Christ!”. The Hegelian, committed to translating this encounter upward into the self-mediation of Reason, is thereby committed to alienating the very person whose experience they claim to comprehend. Keep in mind, this is not a vindication of anti-intellectualism, or an appeal to colloquial understandings, but a clear signal that the disconnect between the systematic “knower” and the so-called simple believer reveals either a fundamental disagreement about the object itself or a total failure of translation. Both of which, exhibit an ineptitude that should be addressed.

Hegel is a Kantian?!?

Regarding the “outrageous” claim that Hegel is a Kantian, it is not my intention to re-inscribe the problems of Kant’s era in calling Hegel a Kantian. He is not Kantian in his conclusions, but in his philosophical gesture. Hegel establishes the absolute sovereignty and constitutive power of thinkability. He completes Kant’s project by collapsing the noumenal/phenomenal divide, but only by making the thinkable (Concept) synonymous with the real. For Kant, the categories of understanding structure our knowledge of phenomena, leaving the thing-in-itself beyond reach. For Hegel, the categories of thought generate the very structure of being itself, and so there is no beyond for thought. However, this triumph comes at a cost identified by the critiques above. The system is a perfectly closed system of thinkable reality, a logical phenomenology. In this, he remains within Kant’s formal constraint; he equates reality with what can be grasped by the (now cosmic, as Steiner calls it) subject’s apparatus, or the global function of thought. This cannot account for the living, creative territory of spiritual beings that precede and exceed, but are still in part constituted by, logical representation. Hegel, in trying to contend with Kantianism, has produced the Absolute Idea, which is just as much a phenomenon contingent on noumena, as Kant’s thing-in-itself.

To end on a high note, a salient point to emphasize is that Steiner does not negate Hegel, but seeks to fulfill him. Their projects share a common origin in the rupture diagnosed by Kant, and a common goal in the realization of human freedom. Both thinkers operate within surprisingly triadic movements: immediacy, mediation, and self mediation. This is the recursive rhythm of life and spirit. For Hegel, this logic culminates in the self-determination of the Absolute Idea. For Steiner, this same logic demands the actual practice of freedom, the development of faculties that allows man to participate consciously in the spiritual being that weaves the world. This is a shared Kantian inheritance. The difference is that Anthroposophy is a call to make the Hegelian system’s conclusion into a starting point. In turning the perfect map of Spirit’s past into a toolkit for navigating the living, creative future, we don’t merely comprehend freedom, but actively achieve it.

  1. Dao De Jing. Chapter 14. Composite translation based on Bruce R. Linnell’s Dao De Jing: A Minimalist Translation, 2015, and Stephen Addiss and Stanley Lombardo’s Lao-tzu: Tao Te Ching, Hackett Publishing, 1993, p. 14. ↩︎

  2. Frye, Northrop. Anatomy of Criticism: Four Essays. Princeton UP, 1957. ↩︎

  3. Hegel, Georg Wilhelm Friedrich. Encyclopedia of the Philosophical Sciences in Basic Outline. Part I: Science of Logic. Specifically, §95 on the https://www.hegel.net website. ↩︎

  4. The Bible. King James Version ↩︎

  5. https://empyreantrail.wordpress.com ↩︎

  6. Goethe, Johann Wolfgang von. “Chorus Mysticus.” Faust, Part Two. Translated by Philip Wayne. Penguin Classics, 1959. LiederNet Archive, www.lieder.net/lieder/get_text.html?TextId=49341. Accessed 10 Dec. 2025. ↩︎

  7. Steiner, Rudolf. Goethes Weltanschauung. Rudolf Steiner Verlag, 1979. Gesamtausgabe (GA), vol. 6.
    —. Die Philosophie der Freiheit. Rudolf Steiner Verlag, 1978. Gesamtausgabe (GA), vol. 4.
    —. Von Jesus zu Christus. Rudolf Steiner Verlag, 1989. Gesamtausgabe (GA), vol. 108. ↩︎

  8. Goethe, Johann Wolfgang von. Die Metamorphose der Pflanzen / The Metamorphosis of Plants. Edited by Gordon L. Miller, Getrud W. Miller, MIT Press, 2009. ↩︎

item.title: Hamann and the Non-Euclidean Revolution of Faith
item.description:
Non-Euclidean Revolution

In a recent unfruitful conversation with Hegelians, I was made to defend the honor of non-Euclidean geometry, despite not being all that invested in the enterprise. The conditions of the argument sought to clarify the character of Non-Euclidean geometry, such that its very possibility was to be established as a whole. The proving grounds for such a position has to come from the conceptual determinations of space. Reason being that geometry is what is known as a science of the Understanding, it is a science with its chief concern being formal validity and symbolic coherence, namely, an avoidance of formal contradiction. No more in geometry does the Euclidean serve as proof that space is Euclidean than Non-Euclidean does. All it does is prove that geometry does not come from observing physical space; it shows that geometry originates in pure thinking rather than empirical observation. Therefore, geometry as a domain exists at a level abstracted beyond the determinations of space. Mathematical systems as a whole are products of the structuring activity of thought, the mind can generate multiple internally consistent “spatial” logics, and so it can be said that mathematics creates conceptual structures by which we engage with (as do all worldviews; see: Mathematism). “Science” then applies these structures to nature, and thus we have the judgment that mathematics models nature, but does not necessarily disclose its essence. It can be said that mathematics is thought grasping pure form independent of sensory experience. Truly, math alone does not penetrate deeper into the spiritual reality of the world, although we will return to this later.

It is evident then, that the possibility of a Non-Euclidean space lies within the determinations of space, which geometry takes as its supposition. Here is where the argument has taken place. Ultimately, the claim I was presented with was that inherent in the immanent development of space was Euclid’s five, yes you heard that right, five postulates, in the form of the claim that dimensions imply orthogonality. Now, I tend to be open to how meaning is disclosed to me. However, the only way in which orthogonality can be bound with dimensionality such that it would prove Euclid’s 5th postulate, is if the argument collapses the distinction between opposed as distinct, or dependent, into orthogonal, meaning at right angles. Orthogonality requires a metric, dimensions are tangent vectors to one another.

The argument leaps from the claim that dimensions are distinct, working at the manifold level, to “therefore perpendiculars exist globally”, working at the metric level. This very assumption is what needs proving, and the reason Euclid could not draw out the fifth postulate from the first four. For Hegel with respect to space, one begins with an immediate and indifferent self-externality. The only interruption of this self-externality can be ideal negations of it. For the purposes of brevity, we may consider point, although it is possible to begin with plane. Points of space are ideal insofar as their negation of extension cannot rest upon any thing in space, because this would be taking the space of that thing for granted. Each point is indifferent and indistinguishable to another, thereby uniting each indifferently with the next. The negative content of both moments is expelled, generating line. Because line is just as indifferent as space, space follows the same pattern from point and line and generates plane. In the words of Richard Dien Winfield, “space’s internal differentiation into point and line entails planes, whose own analogous similitude fills the continuity of space with the unbounded extension of spacial dimensions, which are equally indistinguishable from one another. It makes little difference whether height, length, and breadth be construed in Euclidean space or in a non-Euclidean space, whose use of curvilinear axes already presupposes the two and three-dimensionality defined by straight lines. In each case, what a dimension is identified as is a matter of indifference, since every one can substitute for the others without altering its internal or external relations.” Nothing in the manifold being of space, be it the indifferent distinctions and coexistence of dimensionality, or the for itself indifference of time (it’s ideal logical commencement), implies that a construction of the dimensions involves more than linear independence, not orthogonality. Any more would be an overreach. In my position, the conditions under which one would concede the opposing side would be a proof of the logical necessity of the fifth postulate within the determinations of space. However, as it stands, the conflation between a conceptual opposition, orthogonality, and the parallel postulate is never justified, only asserted. Even Hegel would not make such a bold claim, given that he was highly critical of everything. It also has to be said that in the domain of geometry, the proof has been all but been successful, particularly with Gödel’s incompleteness theorem problematizing, in part, first order axioms and formalist systems, and the explorations of Riemann, Bolyai, and Lobachevsky establish geometries which do not have the fifth postulate holding. Additionally, a position which holds that space as a whole is Euclidean, is a presumptuous position stating that all the work done which is non-Euclidean is either some fluke, intentional misdirection, or worthless. In any case, there is a very prideful and overreaching Moloch behind this claim.

Consequently, the distinction should be made, and has been made by Lawrence S Stepelevich, between what is known as “pure” geometry, and “physical” geometry. Hegel recognized this very distinction, comprehending “what the logic of the understanding has sundered into two exclusive geometries, one developed from an abstract logical content, the other deriving its content from the concrete contingencies of sensuous intuition”. Stepelevich argues that Hegel’s theory of geometry provides speculative support for both Euclidean and non-Euclidean geometry. His critique of Euclid’s congruency postulate in the Science of Logic organizes the distinction:

Thus Euclid’s first propositions about triangles deal only with congruence, that is, how many parts in a triangle must be determined, in order that the remaining parts of one and the same triangle, or the whole of it, shall be altogether determined. The comparison of two triangles with one another, and the basing of congruence on coincidence is a detour necessary to a method that is forced to employ sensuous coincidence instead of the thought, namely, the determinateness of the triangles.

There is a geometry (that, remember, is a level abstracted away from the determinations of space!) which involves the relation between the certainty of the understanding travelling through sensuousness. There is also a geometry which remains in the pure thought of the understanding. The former Stepelevich calls “physical” geometry, and the latter “pure” geometry. What the geometry of the pure understanding provides us with is the fact that the naturality of tridimensional space is taken to hold only for concrete apprehension, for image-thought, and not for the abstract reflections of pure geometry, nor for the determinations of space. An objection might occur here that one expects Hegel to celebrate the intuited three dimensions of space found in “physical geometry” as proof of his method, however, in actuality he rejects this, arguing that geometry is always a level abstracted away from the determinations of space, and, as a science, three-dimensionality itself is not derived from the concept (read: conceptual determinations) of space.

In so far as it is not a philosophical science, geometry may assume the universal determinations of space as its object, and it is not to be demanded of it that it should deduce the necessity of the three dimensions of space.

The differences we perceive between weight, length, and width, are themselves superficial and empty. We cannot define how they differ qualitatively because, at the level of pure thought, they are not yet actual and actualized differences, they are merely supposed to be different. From here, we may return to the establishment of a geometry with this in mind. Pure geometry is the science of abstract conceptual determinations. It deals with the “pure point” (akin to Hilbert’s formal points) which is ens rationis- an entity of reason. The point is once again the negation of space, that in its abstractness is not yet in intuitive space. This pure geometry then continues to describe the internal, necessary articulation of the concept of space as concept. Physical geometry is geometry as it relates to the natural world, to “actual physical mass”. The physical point is a here, a place. The point enters the realm of externality and is thus the “natural concretization” of the pure point. The dimensions (height, length, breadth) appear as the expression of physical mass occupying space. It is not the case that one is true, and the other is false. The culmination of the method in the enclosing surface and the restoration of the spatial totality corresponds to the space of physical bodies. This is a space that has returned to itself, a self-contained whole. This is also the space of “congruence” geometries (such as Euclidean, Spherical, Hyperbolic). These geometries, as Felix Klein systematized them, are concerned with spaces that are homogenous and have properties (such as invariance under translation and rotation), that make them suitable for describing a totality, a world-sphere in which figures can be moved without distortion. The pure mathematics are reflective of a geometry which takes the stages of point, line, and plane as pure moments, representing a space which has not yet achieved totality. They are abstract, one-sided determinations. The “pure point” is not yet in this space or that space. It is the abstract negation that could, in principle, generate any kind of spatial manifold. Both geometries are describing spatiality. Both are, in a sense, necessary. The error would be to mistake the science generated from the abstract moment as being inferior or superior to the science generated from the concrete moment.

Eva Brann’s monumental book, The World of the Imagination: Sum and Substance, offers a historical look at the domain of Euclidean space through an overview of imaginational reality. She inadvertently proves precisely what Hegel claims, that intuitive three-dimensionality is the form of apprehension, and not the content of the concept. Brann merely states it in the form of Euclidean geometry being the imagination’s “native idiom”, yet that theoretical cognition can and does transcend it. Both thinkers agree that Euclidean geometry is how we naturally perceive and imagine space, but it is not a logical necessity and does not exhaust the possibilities of spatiality, thought, and nature. The denial of the possibility of non-Euclidean geometry results from non-speculative thought; such a denial involves the philosophical failure to distinguish between intuition, or immediate imagination, and the concept’s free self-determination. When one asserts that Euclidean space is the only possible geometry, one is unconsciously elevating a report on the imagination’s structure into a limitation on being itself. This is, in essence, a Kantian position, albeit one Kant himself might not have fully endorsed, and one that Hegel’s speculative method definitively overcomes. Euler circles, Venn diagrams, Galilean parabolas, the armillary sphere of the Timaeus, all these are the imagination at work, rendering intelligible structures in intuitively accessible form.

I would like to return to the idea that I am not all invested in the enterprise of non-Euclidean geometry. However, truth-seeker that I am, it is hard to let statements which are blatantly false or mistaken stand, when appropriate to correct them. What I actually believe has very high overlap with Steiner— different geometries correspond to different modes of cognition/perceptions of space. Geometries all describe spatiality by very character of what they are. Geometry provides the quantitative structure of objects furnished from the Understanding’s position as the begetter of thought. As such, Euclidean geometry describes the space we perceive through the senses, it corresponds to waking sensory consciousness, being very accurate for the dead objects of the world; a world of fixed distances, rigid shapes, and so on and so forth. With mathematical thinking, in a geometric proof, Steiner states that “what is present in the mathematical as a conscious fact right up to the lines of reasoning is manageable and accompanied by inner experience from beginning to end”. This is the source of its certainty. The mathematical object is an image which geometry creates, a “pictorial” reality, and in relation to external nature, it is “unreality”. This is also to say that geometry and the determinations of space can never be wholly commensurate. The pure geometry of the understanding is precisely this realm of pictorial unreality. It is thought grasping pure form. So the Euclidean axioms, when modified by a Bolyai or ignored by a Lobachevsky, generate other geometries, they are generating other pictorial worlds from thought. This act of modification is the factual proof of the certainty one feels in the comprehensibility of mathematization. The mind can dwell in these different pictorial spaces with equal rigor and inner consistency.

If Euclidean geometry corresponds to our waking sensory consciousness, being the language for the physical world of mineral bodies, of forces that push and pull centrifugally, it is the geometry of the dead. For this domain, it is perfectly true. However, a “geometry of the living” is necessary. I propose projective geometry. The objects of projective geometry are not abstract objects, or dead objects. Its objects and mathematics describes the formative forces in the world; it is a geometry of process. Steiner himself pointed to the path from analytical to synthetic geometry as a path that leads out of the purely mathematical and towards an encounter with a differentiated, qualitative reality. As he argues, when you follow the implications of projective constructions, you are “forced out of the free formation of the mathematical and into something where the objective passes over into an inner determination, into an inner being”. You are, in essence, beginning to think into the living thing. It is for this reason that I do not put much stock into Euclidean or non-Euclidean geometry. Both have their place and purpose. I much prefer projective geometry for what it can help us visualize and intuit about the world. While more traditional exhibitions of projective geometry exist, such as Lawrence Edwards in The Vortex of Life: Nature’s Patterns in Space and Time, who creates computer generated models of plants, embryos, and organs, like the heart, Annie Besant and Charles Leadbeater’s Thought Forms offers the perfect opportunity for a spiritual-scientific geometry. In the book, they provide numerous illustrations and a clear typology of different forms. If Steiner is right that the respective geometry reflects different modes of cognition/perception of space, then projective geometry, with its focus on mobile, metamorphosing forms (like the conic sections found in Thought Forms), provides the mathematical language for these forces. A growing plant can be understood for the spiritual scientist as a metamorphosis of form that can be modeled by the continuous transformation of a projective curve. Additionally, Steiner’s “intuitive thinking” can help us comprehend the geometry of thought-forms as the visible trace of the etheric formative forces having been impressed upon by a soul quality. The qualitative, inner experience, and the living world of formative, spatial dynamics may provide us with a geometric phenomenon of the soul.

Any further arguments on the intrinsic character of space as “Euclidean” will be met by me with a demand to read Point and Line and Plane by Kandinsky. It is with a great disheartening mood that I must confess: arguments like these are not all that atypical for me when talking with Hegelians. In trying to convey meaning, one will be met with incredulity regarding structures and actualities outside the dogmatic form of the concept. The very argument mentioned has ceded ground to the dogma of the concept- I have not been a Hegelian for a while- and yet in all my efforts to explain why, using analogies and alternative ways of approach, the very approach of analogies and alternatives was the issue. Unless one already has doubts, which are the seeds of escape from the solipsism of the concept, communication will be rendered impossible with the interlocutor. Both thinking and being are restricted, disabling one from accepting that ultimately, we are all analogical. Everything we do and think is in an analogical relationship to the ultimate goodness, of Christ. Rejecting this reality of being and holding onto false Gods is nothing short of fanaticism. The possibility of self-criticism surely always needs to be present, and so my own position in these circumstances must be established. Words and ideas leave psychic imprints on me, such that I can almost “feel” the relationality of what is presented to me through thought. Responsibility must be taken in conveying what is “felt” or “seen” in this structure. However, the limit of accountability is the actions done by other people. In communication, if there is an inability to see what is there, you cannot force someone to see it, just as you cannot force a drug addict to see the good life. Likewise, an inability to afford grace in communication, that the other person is trying to communicate something, something more than their own failure of comprehension, denies the inner life present in all of us, through the grace of God.

Hamann’s Faith

A confession must be made. The debates above reveal an archetype of sorts, of a person sealed inside a circle that has no outside, unable to receive what does not fit the predetermined form. What becomes clear in such moments is that argument alone cannot open what has been closed. Only a burgeoning from the inside may begin to crack the hardened shell of systems-thinking formalism. Something supplementary is needed, a therapy that targets the very aspiration to systemic totality which Hegel inherits from the German Idealist tradition and radicalizes. The person to offer such a therapy is the “Magus of the North”, Johann Georg Hamann.

I have spent years myself inside this circle of circles, I know it’s language, it’s satisfactions, I know it’s Euclidean centrality. And I know what it costs to leave, to slowly recognize that the circle, for all its solidarity, cannot contain what God provides. Relationality is felt, but grace arrives unbidden, a confession must be made:

There is no pure reason. Reason is never abstract, and never universal, nor self-standing. It always belongs to a particular planet, a particular zeitgeist, shaped by a particular language, and tradition. The Hegelian system, for example, for all its claims to absolute knowing, remains immensely humanist: it can only ever be a relationality that is intelligible to us, not to a relationality to the object itself. What sense would it make to speak of a relationality we have no relationship with? The very question lends us the answer.

We approach this limit constantly through analogy. The relationship between God and humanity, the divine and the human, is an analogical one, based on the free act of God in the Incarnation. The union of two natures in Christ is a mystery to be believed and adored, rather than something to be reconciled by speculative reason. The relationship is open because the kind of operative protocol the concept undergoes is incapable of getting from the human to the divine. There is a gap that can only be crossed by a gift from the other side. The attempt to expunge reason of its contingency is the proton pseudos of systematicity and the beginning of the neglect of faith. Reason’s purpose is to reveal its own limits. Like the law in Pauline theology, reason is a pedagogue. It leads us to the end of our own resources, exhausting its own resources until what is left is what is. Surely, Hegel’s methodology can be described as a series of self-exhaustions of the system itself. But for Hegel, this exhaustion leads to an ever higher integration, an apotheosis of reason. For Hamann, it leads elsewhere entirely. Reasons self-critique is meant to show us our ignorance and need for something beyond ourselves. When reason claims to be sufficient, it becomes idolatrous. Reason itself is a fallen creature, a fallen human capacity. It is given sensations, given a tradition, given a language, and ultimately given revelation. Its job is to acknowledge this dependence, not to spin everything out of itself. Thus, reason’s ultimate purpose is to lead us to the end of our own resources. It is meant to fail, to show us the abyss. This is the exact opposite of Hegel’s project, where reason’s self-examination leads to its own apotheosis. For Hamann, reason’s examination leads only to its own limit. What lies beyond that limit is not for reason to say.

Perhaps what lies beyond that limit, what is given is language. In the beginning was the Word, after all. Hamann has the view that language is prior to reason. To an Idealist, this would seem like a crude, empirical assertion which the concept would sublate (yes I will use that word). They would almost immediately assert: “Prior? prior in what sense? Chronologically? Empirically? This would not make sense, given it would be anthropological, rather than philosophical. Spirit sublates such empirical conditions into its own self-conscious logical development. The truth of language is the Concept!”.

Hamann is certainly not making a claim about cavemen grunting before being able to think. He is making a humbler claim about the nature of all thought- that language is the inescapable medium of all thought. You cannot get behind it or beyond it to a purer realm of thought, whether that realm is Kant’s transcendental unity of apperception, or Hegel’s pure self-thinking thought. The idea of a thought that has fully sublated its linguistic expression into a pure, self-present meaning is an illusion. Steiner himself has commented on how a spiritual person like Hamann would be quick to jump against the position that meaning is not immanent, or located within some “outside”. He would likely say to Hegel’s Concept, if one were to hold the position that the form is “sublated” (and in some sense contingent to the word, whether it be the rational natural contingency of form, or the formalism of the Understanding which only values symbolic coherence) that for all its claimed concreteness it is just another attempt to vault over the abyss of language’s being, which is itself being.

Hamann often uses the term “hysteron proteron” to accuse both Kant, and by extension, any idealist system of getting things backwards. Such a position would treat the a priori (the logical, conceptual) as if it were the foundation, when in fact it is derived from and always dependent upon the a posteriori. The genealogical priority of language means that what philosophy claims is first (the Concept) is actually “last”. Or as scripture puts it: so the last shall be first, and the first last. The very possibility of an abstract logical determination purifying itself of its linguistic and embodied skin is denied by Hamann. There is no thought before or outside language. There is only thought in language, shaped by it and carried by it. Consider a practical example. What is gender? One approach might attempt a rational determination: gender is logically rooted in the sexed distinction, a clean conceptual definition which admits no variability (although it certainly leaves room for it). This approach, for all its claim to necessity, tells you nothing about what gender actually is! It is indistinguishable from a dogmatic traditionalism that simply asserts the union of gender and sex. The alternative is to attend to how gender appears through particular cultures, how it is lived, and spoken, and named. This second approach does not begin with the concept and apply it to the world. It begins with the world, with language and tradition, and lets the concept emerge from there. The concept, in this case, is not the foundation, but the result. It is last, not first. This is essentially Hamann’s point. Language is not conceived of as the clothing of the thought, it is its body. You cannot strip it away to find the pure concept underneath, because there is nothing underneath. The concept is not behind the word, it is in the word, inseparable from it, given with it. And this is why reason, which thought itself sovereign, must confess its dependence. Reason does not invent language for itself, it is born into language, it relates the word to itself. It does not master language, it is mastered by it. Reason is pedagogue to Christ. The ultimate purpose of reason and its self-critique is to lead us to the end of our own resources. It is meant to fail, to show us the “abyss,” so that we might stop trusting in it and instead trust in the Word that is given to us. This is the exact opposite of Hegel’s project, where reason’s self-examination leads to its own apotheosis, what Steiner calls logical fetishism (for more reasons than this).

There are some relations you have to take on faith, relations which reason itself cannot immediately show you. For example, it is inconceivable to see karmic links between people within a framework like Kant’s or Hegel’s. The Incarnation is another example. In Christ, divine and human natures unite without fusion, without one side being reduced to the other. You cannot arrive at the self-relationality of Christ by reasoning, because this system of relationality is not exhaustive of Christ’s self-relationality, it is only exhaustive of the intelligibility reason has. This is why faith is not a substitute for reason, nor a moment to be subsumed within reason’s development. Faith is the mode of relating to what exceeds us- to the God who gives himself, to the Word that speaks to us, and to the relations that hold us together, the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit. Faith is the trust that the gift is good, and that the giver is faithful. In the future this mode of relation will develop itself into a new faculty. Nevertheless, this will be for a future time, brought along by a revolution as paradigm shifting as non-Euclidean geometry was for the Kantians.

item.title: Man, Animal, and Evolution: The Relationship Finally Clarified
item.description:
Intro

After writing my recent article Epistle to Polygenism, a few insights came to mind regarding the human form and it’s arrangement, going hand in hand with an elaboration of the relationship between man and animals. This article is on this consideration. In this undertaking, the process describing the generation of embodied form these beings take is necessary, namely, an explication of “evolution”, which is the name for this process. In its conventional usage, “evolution” refers generally to the gradual physical transformation of species over geological time, driven by variations and natural selection (which is a catch-all term employed to describe a failure for an organism to reach biotic potential). This framework has provided the world with a few insights, surely, yet it leaves more basic questions unanswered. Why did self-consciousness emerge only in one lineage? What is the inner relationship between the human form and the animal forms that so closely resemble it? Why does the fossil record show long periods of status punctuated by sudden appearances, rather than the smooth continuum the theory might predict?

To address these, we must expand our understanding of evolution to include dimensions that the physical sciences, by their very methodology, cannot access. Spiritual-scientific research offers precisely such an expansion. In this account, evolution is not merely the history of bodily forms. It involves the evolution of the planet, of spirits, or the ideas and concepts by which perceptions are generated by, the whole of nature, and so on and so forth. Thus far, the only theory in materialistic sciences approaching such a conception is the Expanding Earth hypothesis, which can explain everything modern plate tectonics does except for not taking as an assumption the fixed radius of the Earth over time. In addition, it also answers questions such as the relatively young age of the ocean floor, and a few others. Besides this theory, modern materialist science treats nature as dead, and if not dead, then it is dead at the moment of observation, put into hypostasis for the thinking subject. Evolution as properly understood involves the spiritual hierarchies who guide cosmic and earthly development, dimensions of evolution such as Conditions of Life, Form, and Consciousness, planetary stages, and involution and sacrifice wherein higher spiritual beings descend into lower states to create conditions for humanity’s ascent. Evolution can be said to be sustained by a cosmic sacrifice. Within this framework, the relationship between humanity and the natural kingdoms, for instance, does not appear to us as one of linear emergence. It is a stratified separation. Telos is also involved. If something is developing, there is something it develops towards, and so the spiritual science’s understanding of evolution does not end with the present, nor does it end with biological organisms. Everything in nature is alive, as Schelling states. However, this will be elaborated on at a further date. The question concerning us today is along the following: Why do we look like apes?

Monkey

Humans and great apes have a strikingly similar skeletal structure. The arrangement of limbs, the opposable thumbs, the facial musculature, and so on, have long been held up as the crowning evidence for Darwinian evolution. When Huxley, Darin’s “bulldog”, famously toured the British Isles with a chimpanzee skeleton, his message was very clear. The human body is merely a modified ape body according to him. The resemblance is so close that comparative anatomy leaves little room for doubt. We share a common ancestor, the ape form is our past, and the human form is a more refined variation of the same basic blueprint, problem solved. This argument has become the bedrock of modern evolutionary biology.

Note: The entire field of evolution tends to be pseudo-science, lacking all forms of explanatory value or normativity. Whether evolutionary psychology or biology, it does not matter. What a trait in one organism is said to be an adaptation, the respective opposite trait in another is also said to be a beneficial adaptation. For instance, a dull color in a bird may be beneficial in order for it to survive and reach biotic potential (reproduce). Within the same species bright-colored feathers is explained away as beneficial for attracting mates and reaching biotic potential. If any trait can be rationalized post hoc, its explanatory power becomes null. More on this: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Just-so_story. While the counterargument to this statement is that an emphasis on different selective pressures resulting in fitness is the main point might seem attractive, further probing reveals that fitness (survival and reproduction) becomes a tautological loop of reproduction. “What reproduces is more fit, and the fittest survive” becomes “those reproduce more reproduce more”. Evolutionary psychology is similarly a fruitless domain parading itself as normative theory.

The resemblance of monkey to man is elegant, parsimonious, and seemingly supported by a wealth of genetic and fossil data. Yet for all its explanatory power, it still rests on the assumption that morphological similarity necessarily implies genealogical descent. In other words, because we look like apes, we must have evolved from them. This is an unjustified assumption that will find no solution in the materialistic sciences. Modern neo-Darwinian synthesis is ultimately a theory of form. It explains how populations adapt to environments through variation and natural selection, supposedly. There are disagreements, but we can grant this for the sake of argument. We may also grant that it successfully accounts for the diversification of species, the distribution of traits, and the patterns of genetic relatedness. The human-ape similarity fits into this framework, that we share a primate body because we inherited it from a primate ancestor, with modifications that accumulated along separated linages. However, the limit is reached precisely at the threshold of what makes us human. The aforementioned theory cannot explain why, among all primates, only one lineage developed self-consciousness, the capacity for abstract thought, moral reflection, language, art, and so on. It treats these as emergent epiphenomena of increased brain size, yet it has no account of why the brain expanded in this lineage alone, or how mere quantity of neurons could ever produce the qualitative leap to the reflexive “I”. The evolutionary biologist is left with what Thomas Nagel coined “the hard problem” of consciousness. This is a problem that neo-Darwinian synthesis and natural selection was never equipped to solve.

Additionally, Darwinism (as shorthand) treats the animal kingdom as a series of steps leading *coincidentally to man. The ape is positioned as our nearest relative, a kind of unfinished version of ourselves. This narrative implicitly carries a hierarchy, but it cannot ground that hierarchy in anything beyond differential reproductive success. Morality, art, philosophy, and language all become byproducts of a brain that happened to get larger, with an unclear object in mind, and in some cases denies the object is there at all. The human form is reduced to a contingent outcome of mutation and selection, with no intrinsic meaning or direction.

Spiritual science offers a fundamentally different reading of the same anatomical facts. In this view, the human being is the original form. Not in the sense of a physical ancestor, but as an archetypal spiritual reality that preceded the physical separation of the kingdoms of nature. Long ago, during periods of times named the “Lemurian” and “Atlantean” epochs, the human physical body was still malleable and had not yet solidified into the fixed skeletal form we know today. At various stages of this long process of solidification, certain beings that guide species from the astral plane (called group souls) took hold of physical bodies that had prematurely hardened. These bodies, which had once been part of the human stream, became the foundations for the animal kingdom. The apes represent the last such separation, occurring when the human form was already quite close to its final shape. This is why their anatomy so closely resembles ours. They are a kind of photograph of the human form at the moment it branched away. In this conception, a portion of developing humanity “lags behind” and becomes the basis for a new natural kingdom. The animals, plants, and minerals are not our ancestors; they are our own former possibilities frozen in time.

The ape, therefore, is not a primitive ancestor, but a side branch. It is a being that took on the physical form that the human stream had at a particular stage, but then stopped developing further. The anatomical similarity cannot be evidence that we evolved from apes, it is instead evidence that apes and humans share a common origin in the plastic proto-human form which existed before the final hardening of the human body.

If this reading is correct, then the fossil hominins (australopithecines, Homo habilis, Homo erectus, and even earlier forms) take on a different significance, representing various side branches or transitional forms that either preceded the full descent of the human “I” into physical bodies, or were left behind as the human form continued to refine and harden. As mentioned in my Polygenism article, the fact that stone tools appear with australopithecines shows that even before the “I” fully incarnated, beings with a degree of intelligence and will were interacting with the physical world. These beings, in Steiner’s account, were ensouled by group souls (or by individual souls not yet fully independent) who were preparing the physical “body” for the eventual incarnation of the self-conscious, reflexive “I”. The appearance of anatomically modern Homo sapiens in the Quaternary period (precisely when the Out of Africa population emerges) marks the completion of the hardening process. The physical body is stably mineralized and capable of hosting the “I”.

Conventional evolutionary theory, exactly because it limits itself to physical form and a vague notion of genetic inheritance, cannot accommodate the above picture. It has no language for telos, ideality, freedom, self-determination, etc. Yet these are precisely the dimensions needed to answer the questions that Darwinism leaves open. Why does consciousness exist? Why only in humans? Ultimately, what is the relationship between the human form and the animal form that surrounds us? Spiritual science certainly does not reject the empirical findings of paleontology, genetics, taxonomy studies, or comparative anatomy. Instead, it offers a broader view for these findings, one that interprets the same data in a fuller way and recontextualizes the information accordingly in a unified way. The ape-human similarity then transforms from an animal origin to a human origin.

Influence and Development

In order to understand the human-animal relationship, let us set aside the image of evolution as a linear chain from simple to complex, of ape to man. In its place, we must envision a process. Rationalize this process however you want; the Hegelian abstract to concrete, the alchemical process, separation to hardening to inward transformation, etc. are ways to understand the procedural moments here. This is a process that spans beyond the history of the Earth as we currently understand it into the period of time known as the middle Lemurian epoch (roughly 359–299 million years ago). If the “archetype” of man was abstract (spiritual, so to say) at some point, then this is the period it began to “harden” and manifest a body in the world for itself (in addition to the beginning of the sex distinction, among others). The human form was not yet the fixed structure we know today. Steiner describes it as “plastic” often, “soft”, and still directly shaped by the will. Bones had not yet fully mineralized, the body was more fluid, more responsive to the inner life of the soul, as a necessary condition for the incarnation of the human “I” in the world. During this epoch, the human being moved through the world with a body that was, in essence, a sculpture in process. The forces that would later become the distinct animal, plant, and mineral kingdoms were still contained within the human form. The human being was, as Steiner puts it, a kind of “potential totality” from which all other terrestrial forms would eventually be separated. It is worth noting that the Earth and man concurrently “evolve”, however, we are focused on man and animals for now.

The Atlantean epoch continued this process. The body remained more fluid than today. For example the skeletal system was still not fully solid. Yet a progressive hardening was underway. The first really durable artifacts appear only at the very end of Atlantis, marking the threshold where human will began to encounter a world that was itself becoming mineralized. The great disaster and flood that ended Atlantis finalized this transition. The human form, now sufficiently hardened, could enter the current post-Atlantean epoch as a stable vessel for the fully incarnated “I”. Why did the human form need to harden exactly? Because the “I” requires a respective stable instrument. A body that is too plastic cannot reliably support the kind of self-conscious reflection that defines true humanity. The hardening of the skeleton, the mineralization of the bones, the stabilization of the brain structure, etc. are the invented culmination of a long spiritual preparation. The fossil record, when read this way, reflects this gradual solidification. The reflexive, self-conscious human “I” did not always dwell in a physical body though. During earlier planetary stages of Earth’s development, the human soul moved among the spiritual hierarchies, gradually preparing for embodiment. The descent into a physical form in one sense is considered a sacrificial act. The higher “I” bound itself to a body that would limit it, in order for it to gain the capacity for freedom, self-knowledge, love, etc. This descent did not happen all at once. In the Lemurian epoch, the “I” began to inhabit the still plastic human form, but was not yet fully integrated. During the Atlantean epoch, the “I” deepened its connection, but still the body was not entirely stable. Only in our epoch did the “I” achieve full-self consciousness within a fully hardened body.

This brings us to today. If the animal kingdom consists of forms that separated from the human stream at various stages, then the human being accurately is more than the synthesis of animal traits as a being who inwardly transformed what animals externalized. This is the principle of metamorphosis which Goethe recognized in plants where leaf transforms into sepal, petal, stamen, etc., and in animals, where the vertebrae transformed into skull bones. This extends into the whole of evolution. The human form is a metamorphosed unity of what appears in the animal kingdom as separate, specialized organs.

Consider the fact that what in animals is an external organ becomes an internal organ in humans. The fish’s swimming bladder, which regulates buoyancy, is metamorphosed into the human eardrum. The fish’s gills become the tiny bones of the ear. What was once spread out in water becomes concentrated and internalized. What in animals is a specialized function becomes a unified capacity in humans. Steiner talks in depth on the siphonophore, a colony of distinct individuals each performing a single function such as balance, feeding, movement, reproduction, or defense. This is a kind of deconstructed human. In the human being, all these functions are drawn together into a single self-conscious organism, held together by the “I”. When the “I” fails to hold them together, we see the fragmentations of mental illness, a kind of return, in distorted form, to the condition of the siphonophore. One may also consider that a one-sided development becomes a balanced threefoldness in humans. Animals are, as Steiner says, “human beings which have remained stationary in the one-sided development of their characteristics”. The ant has developed social organization to an extreme, but lacks individuality. The beaver has developed engineering wisdom, but cannot innovate. The human being, in contrast, holds thinking, feeling, and willing, in a balance that requires the insecurity and openness that the one-sided animal lacks.

This inward transformation certainly didn’t happen automatically. It was guided by spiritual beings working from the astral plane. Since animals don’t have an “I”, they are guided by animal group-souls, beings on the astral plane who direct the collective intelligence of the species (think the beaver’s dam, the bird’s nest or migration, and the bee’s hive). The third elementary Kingdom works directly on animal forms, shaping them according to instincts and desires. In the human being, these formative forces are internalized. The same forces that shape the animal from without become, in the human, the inner forces of conscience and self-direction. In other words, the human “I” learns to do consciously what the group soul does instinctively.

The ape occupies a special place in this picture. It separated from the human stream last, when the human form was already close to its final shape. Its anatomy is therefore most similar to ours for this reason, because it froze at the stage just before the human form achieved its full uprightness, the capacity to receive the “I”, and so on. Moreover, Steiner notes that the ape “brings too much from the group soul down into its own individual existence”. Unlike lower animals, which fully return to the group soul at death, the ape retains something in its physical organization that cannot go back. This gives it a quality that appears almost individual- a something that resembles the human “I”, but is in fact a stalled potentiality. This residue becomes, in the spiritual world, the elementals called salamanders, beings that are neither fully human, nor fully animal, lingering as a kind of unfinished creation.

Everything is for a purpose or, the form of man

The animal kingdom consisting of potentials separated from man is on the one hand. The other hand focuses on what the animal kingdom is a derivation of: the human form. Then human form can thus be understood as the living synthesis of these potentials, more than the sum of the parts. It contains these potentials by having turned them inside out, under the sovereignty of the “I”. Among all features that distinguish the human body, three stand out as especially revealing. First is the upright posture, then the articulate larynx, and finally, the free hand with its opposable thumb. Each of these is a metamorphosed animal organ, an inward transformation of what in the animal kingdom appears as an external structure.

The most visible difference between humans and the higher animals is the vertical orientation of the spine. In the animal, the spine runs horizontally. The head is attached at an angle, its weight being carried by the forelimbs. In the human being, the spine stands upright, and the head balances freely atop it. For conventional biology, this is a simple and mere addendum to bipedalism, one that freed the hands for tool use. For spiritual science, that which subsumes the material sciences, it is the physical expression of the “I”. Steiner explains that the upright posture serves as a necessary condition for self-consciousness, in addition to it being a mechanical advantage. When the spine is horizontal, the astral body flows predominantly in the direction of the Earth’s surface, anchoring consciousness in instinct. But when the spine becomes vertical, the astral body can orient itself toward the cosmos, allowing the “I” to descend into the physical body. The upright human being stands as a column connecting heaven and earth, as a microcosmic counterpart to the cosmic order.

The uprightness is also the basis for the threefold division of the human organism into the nervous system, rhythmic system, and metabolic limb-system mentioned in a previous article on the development of human physiology and spirituality. Each of these three systems have their own relation to the animal kingdom, meaning in the animal, these functions are often spatially separated into distinct organs, or even distinct creatures. In the human being, they are drawn together into a single upright form, integrated by the “I”. The upright posture makes possible a second transformation via the metamorphosis of the air-organism into the organ of speech.

Consider a bird. Its entire organization is oriented toward the air, with its hollow bones, its lungs connected to air sacs extending throughout the body. The bird’s song is produced by a syrinx that vibrates with the breath. It is, in a way, an instrument of the air elementals, being shaped by the element it moves through. The song can be considered an instinctive yet direct expression of the group soul. During the Lemurian epoch, when the human form was still plastic, one possible evolutionary path was to become a bird-like being. Had the human stream talent that direction, we would have become creatures of the air, our whole organization lightened and specialized for flight, and our consciousness, merged with the astral currents of the atmosphere.
