So paradoxically, the biological female became the physical home for the active, masculine principle of Will in its outgoing expression. However, her enduring “core”, the formative ground of her consciousness, remained rooted in the imaginative, feminine aspect (Kriya Shakti) from which she originated. The other side of the coin has the male body specializing as the vessel for internalizing Imagination (the feminine principle). With the loss of the old dreamlike consciousness, the soul’s female aspect could no longer project itself freely onto a malleable world. It required a new, protected, interior space. The male body then became the locus for this forced internalization. The rigorous training in “hardening” and endurance suppressed the external, sensual expression of will (which was now more the domain of the female form) and thereby dam up the creative, formative energy. This dammed-up, inward (and upward) rushing force was the substance that built the interiority, the refined nervous system, and the physical brain. Thought is the off spring of this internalized imagination, it is Kriya Shakti turned inward on itself, generating abstract ideation. Therefore, the biological male became the home for the internalized, formative principle of imagination (the feminine principle). Yet, just like the female, his foundational soul principle remained the Will (Iccha Shakti), the force he was tasked to apply from within. There is a structural cross-polarization here. The female possesses a body specialized as an instrument of externalized Will, while her soul is at core Imagination. The male possesses a body specialized as an instrument of internalized imagination (the thinking organism) while his soul is at core Will. There is a structural tension present here, where the soul calls from one pole, but the body is tooled for its opposite. Between these two ends is the makings of an individual consciousness, and a telos geared towards holistic integration.

From another perspective, this fact of “man in woman and woman in man” is almost common sense. Fertilization by the Spirit (the previously mentioned process of the inward turning of creative energy which created our capacity for thought) makes us whole, insofar as we seek a conscious integration of the other polarity, finding its correlate in the spiritual task of becoming whole. However, all pretenses to “being a man” and “being a woman” are somewhat trivialized and revealed as superficial performances once one recognizes that has never been a “100%” feminine or masculine. This is evident on multiple levels. Biologically, each human being is a genetic mosaic of male and female potentiality. Physiologically, both sexes produce “opposite” hormones (testosterone in women, estrogen in men), of course in quantities essential to health and equilibrium. We are, in a way, walking polarities. This inherent bisexuality extends into the realm of character as well. There exists no pure “manly” will utterly devoid of receptive imagination, nor a purely “feminine” imagination completely free of directive will. The most decisive leader requires an intuitive empathy to guide them effectively, and even the most nurturing caregiver must summon formidable will in order to protect and provide. What we label as masculine or feminine traits are temporary accents of one pole. As of now, this narrative, or at the very least the insights gained from it, does not invent a duality, but it does provide a teleological explanation for it. Our inherent mixed state is the direct result and proof of the old split. Consequently, rigid gender roles and identities are revealed as evolutionary scaffold. I can see the argument made for them, perhaps for a time, to cultivate the specialized strengths of each pole, but ultimately they are meant to be outgrown. In fact, the moment they are to be outgrown is the moment they are grasped in their totality inwardly. The spiritual task of the current epoch is precisely beyond hinging individual “I” identity as a man or a woman, in any exclusive sense of the label. It is in recognizing ourselves as conscious Spirits learning to marry both principles within.

What follows below are various notes related to the topic of sexuality that I hope can serve one well.

Gender

What is gender? Is it a socially mediated reality? Is it something irreducible to a biological given? The question of gender can only be answered after having answered the question of sex and sexual differentiation. Sex refers to the biological differentiation into male and female physical bodies. It is a phenomenon of the current Earth epoch, specifically arising from the middle of the Lemurian period onward. We find its origin as the result of the “hardening” of matter. As the Earth’s substance condensed, the originally androgynous, plastic human body could no longer express the fully bipolar soul. It split into two specialized physical forms. Sex is a temporary, necessary sacrifice for the sake of the evolution of man. The male and female bodies are very specialized instruments, each optimized for a specific cosmic, or global, function. The female body is structured to channel forces outward into the world, where the male body is an instrument for internalizing the Imagination principle. It is structured to dam up creative spiritual force and turn it inward to build up the brain and intellectual capacity. Sex, insofar as it is a biological reality, has two important aspects to its reproduction. As it relates to the reproduction of the “self”, it represents further breaks from external cosmic forces and beings and takes a more independent posture for itself. Seeing as we are not fully independent, we are still linked to the Earth as of now. As it relates to the reproduction of the being in the form of population, the two seemingly counterposed bodies require each other in order to make another. Physical sexuality is a transitory stage. It will eventually be transcended as humanity spiritualizes, with future stages involving the return of a form of spiritual androgyny (the Moon’s return leading to new modes of being).

Gender, on the other hand, has two senses of the word. The first refers to a universal sense of differentiation, which predates the sexed distinction. Think of the term “engendering”, wherein a thing begets another thing. Or we can also consider it as “genus” in a spiritual sense. Just as in biology, the genus Felis differentiates into species (lions, tigers), in spiritual evolution, the primal human archetype differentiates into various genders, or genera of life (avian, mammalian, etc.) before the human being itself crystallizes a distinct spiritualized entity. Thus, man engenders the animal kingdom. Differentiation is a universal principle. Likewise, the later male/female split within humanity is a higher, more conscious summa of this same principle, now applied to the human form for a moral and spiritual purpose.

The second sense is the one we will find most familiar. It is the two universal spiritual principles of Will (Masculine) and Imagination (Feminine), or a sense of “archetypal” differentiation. In other words, it is the polarization of the unified human archetype into the masculine and feminine principles and their subsequent cross-polarized incarnation into specialized male and female bodies. This can be considered a “sub-category” of the first sense. Human gender is the most advanced, conscious, and for us at least, spiritually significance instance of the universal sense of differentiation. While the “bird gender” separated to explore the principle of air/lightness/thought, human gender separation was for the purpose of generating self-consciousness and moral freedom.

Cultural expressions of gender are external reflections of the internal, spiritual polarity, filtered through the consciousness of a specific epoch and people. They are soul-led interpretations of the cosmic masculine/feminine principles. Atlantean cultural expression found a direct reflection of cosmic assignment. Women were the natural priestesses, or the interpreters of nature’s will. Men were the technicians and will-driven actors. In Post-Atlantean cultures (current), the awakening of the “I” and the intellectual mind saw the nigh omniscient memory capabilities of the past and direct perception of the spiritual principles fade away gradually. Cultures now interpret and conceptualize the gender polarity. Patriarchal cultures elevate the externalized principle of Will (law, conquest, abstract sky-god) to conscious ideals. This corresponds to the current epoch’s task of developing the masculine principle of conscious thought and agency. Matrifocal cultures may preserve more intuitive memory of the feminine cosmic principle. Luciferic or Ahrimanic cultures constitute a distortion of these principles. A Luciferic culture might exalt masculine will into arrogant individualism, or feminine imagination into a kind of hysteric sentimentality. An Ahrimanic culture might reduce masculine thought to a sterile materialism, and respectively feminine receptivity to passive collectivism. Any mix and match of these possibilities is possible.

With respect to practical shorthand, when encountering any given culture’s gender expression, we should first look for the underlying spiritual principles of “masculine and feminine” as they are emphasized, then contextualize this expression both historically and spiritually as an interpretation fitted to a specific culture’s particularity. Then, we must appraise its spiritual health. Does it foster a one-sided development, or does it reflect an arrogant individualism and the like? We should hold all such expressions non-absolutely, remembering that they are temporary “experiments” on the path towards the transcendence of the polarity itself, while retaining a respect for the fact of this differentiation. This perspective moves us beyond the typical “nature versus nurture” debates (and even the fence sitting nuance-mongers who say “it’s both” without providing a telos or actual account of how it’s both beyond providing a mutual conditioning), into a perspective that covers all bases. It provides us a concept of gender expression in culture where it is rightfully seen as filled with soul as an imperfect attempt to work with the cosmic principles, using human duality as a justification for spiritual advancement.

The Family Structure

Each family role embodies a specific spiritual principle that mirrors cosmic processes. Hegel has stated that the “structure of family is the structure of mind” and such. I am in broad agreement, with one qualification: he doesn’t have a telos of gender and sex, and likewise cannot possibly have license to foresee the expansion of family into a family of all mankind, where all members freely recognize themselves as such.

The mother represents receptivity, the father represents activity, and the child represents the future and the incoming spiritual world. The family’s duty is to provide a healthy vessel and a morally sound environment so that this spirit can fulfill its mission. Education is considered a task of cooperating with the child’s own spiritual guidance.

The modern family structure is quite a relatively new phenomenon. In ancient times, individuality was weak. People identified with a group soul or clan consciousness. “Family” was the entire tribe, and leadership was by spirit (through initiates and priestesses). Procreation was guided by spiritual beings, and not personal choice. With the solidification of the “I” in the Post-Atlantean age, the family became centered on blood kinship and lineage. This was the emergence of the bloodline. It was also a necessary stage for the individuation of the ego. I would write a mini-thesis on the French Revolution here, but this post has gone on long enough. What I mean to emphasize is that ancestor worship and patriarchal lineages reflected a growing importance of inherited physical and soul qualities. However, seeing as our current fifth Post-Atlantean epoch (since around the 15th century) has had its task of individualization and freedom, the tight bonds of “bloodline” are loosened. The modern family, based on personal love and choice (e.g.; I choose to love this person and bring another person into the world), rather than obligation to clan is the result. The purpose of the modern nuclear family is to become a kind of school for free and moral relationships; where love between parents is a conscious spiritual act and decision, and raising children is a service to the independent spirit of the child. The foundation of the family structure is marriage, with the deepest foundation of marriage being the sanctified polarity between masculine and feminine principles, which each partner works to harmonize within themselves and between each other. Two souls are drawn together by spiritual laws in order to balance shared karma, or fulfill mutual obligations from past lives, or provide the specific conditions for each other’s further development. It is also a conscious reunion sanctioned by an institution, that is, in marriage, the two halves of male and female seek each other. Their attraction is the archetypical fulfillment of a reconstituted being. The spouse is the physical embodiment of the externalized opposite of one’s own soul. For man, he meets in his wife the externalized form of will, which is the tool he must learn to spiritualize. She is his power, and his living connection to active power in the world. For the woman, she meets in her husband the externalized form of internalized Imagination, which is the tool she must learn to spiritualize. He is her “source” and her living connection to a structured consciousness. Through the loving, conscious interplay of this polarity, each partner helps the either redeem and integrate their own inner opposite. The husband, through his wife, learns to imbue his thinking with warm, moral will. The wife, through her husband, learns to direct her formative forces with clarity. Together, they enact the creative act on a human scale. Marriage can be taken to be the highest human art. It is the most demanding but most rewarding spiritual practice accessible to most.

Homosexuality and Transgenderism

Any critical look at homosexuality should grasp the more ideal principles from which the idea generates itself. This includes moving beyond the “telos” of natural immediacy (which often times is just biological determinism). What this implies for homosexuality, is that it can be defined as a karmically significant asymmetry between the spiritual and the physical. Normatively, it is positioned as an incarnational discord that requires resolution. The structure is determined following the eternal human spirit (which bears a qualitative bias toward either the masculine or feminine principles) which incarnates into a physical sexed body. Homosexuality then arises when this spiritual being seeks to reconcile its internal polarity outside the prescribed biological normativity, attracting toward the same sex as a mirror of its own spiritual gender. Structurally, this constitutes a bypassing of the intended domain of duality, where the male-female reproductive duality is the normative vehicle for soul evolution in the current epoch. Teleologically, it is therefore classified as a transitory developmental phenomenon, or as a suboptimal configuration that appears as humanity refines its engagement with polarity. This must eventually and ultimately be overcome as the spirit progresses toward the destined state of conscious, spiritual androgyny. Procreative duality is the intended path for human evolution. Yes, this means “marriage” is between man and woman. Same-sex attraction is spiritually meaningful, but ultimately a provisional detour.

“Transgenderism” is also positioned here as a meaningful karmic configuration. What might be diagnosed as gender dysphoria, or gender identity disorder, is interpreted as a direct carry-over of soul memory from a previous incarnation. There is the possibility here that it could stand as the remembrance of the soul of its androgynous period of time, however, various studies which find that children recalling a past-life personality of the opposite sex exhibited significantly higher rates of gender non-conformity. Look to the work of Ian Stevenson and the University of Virginia Division of Perceptual studies here. Empirically, this supports a remise that the soul’s history directly shapes present-life identity, which is much more in line with an anthroposophical outlook, than the dogmatic assertion that the soul is reverting to androgyny. For that reason, a persistent transgender identification is normatively determined as the soul’s conscious (or subconscious) retention of its previous incarnation’s gendered experience and spiritual orientation. This presents a configured mismatch- a soul is strongly imprinted with, for example, masculine spiritual attributes through numerous male lifetimes incarnates into a female body, leading to an innate sense of alienation from the physical instrument, and a compelled reinterpretation of cultural gender roles. We do not have time to go too in-depth on spiritual economy, and the principles of reincarnation, but what I mean to convey with this scenario is that the condition in the abstract is a deliberate karmic task. The soul, among other things, must consciously reconcile its enduring internal identity with the contrary spiritual lessons and social expectations of its new biological form, working toward a higher unity that advances both persona evolution, and humanity’s particular (which is also its universal) movement beyond a fixed physical polarity.

Now, I emphasize in the “abstract”, because we don’t live in the abstract, in itself world. There are influences which nudge one towards one direction or another. I mean to say that it is not a coincidence that we see the rise of transgender and homosexual advocacy at the same time as the dominance of propaganda structures, marketing firms, cosmetic and pharmaceutical industries, and so on and so forth. The mutual emergence of these things is a manifestation of Ahrimanic and Luciferic influences working upon and through these authentic soul conditions. Luciferic and Ahrimanic forces do not create these identities from nothing, they do not have the capability to truly “create”, as they are one side of the “duality” of creation (this is very vague, perhaps I will write about it in a future article). These forces actively amplify, distort, and instrumentalize in that order, for their own ends, pulling them away from their potential spiritual purposes. Luciferic influences operate through propaganda, marketing, and “digital media” (which takes its form via the internet in this era, but in future eras… perhaps I have said too much…) to inflate identity into the rebellious, absolutist ideology of the ego, or of subjectivism. It glorifies the individual’s experience into a supreme, uncompromising truth (known as Luciferic pride), cleaving it from any connection to biological reality, or higher karmic contexts, or even collective spiritual development. What it encourages is a hysterical self-creation wherein it says “you, the subject, the only subject, can be anything!”. There is a divorce of identity from connection to cosmic principles, and subsequently duty. Likewise, all traditional structures are seen as nothing but oppressive illusions. The various isms and identities one sees online and in real life, especially through the second-order medium of gender ideology (I am asexual, I am pansexual, I am a “MAP”. Although it must be stated, this Luciferic influence is not limited to gender ideology, it also exists in philosophy, particularly in moral designations. “I am a utilitarian, I am a deontologist, I am a virtue ethicist”, and so on and so forth). The Ahrimanic influence operates through the pharmaceutical and the surgical through the bureaucratic-corporate state apparatus to mechanize, commodify, and ultimately reduce these identities. It aims to frame the higher soul condition of gender dysphoria as a straightforward biochemical or surgical engineering problem to be solved by hormone blockers, surgery, and a lifelong dependence on pharmaceutical dependence. The transformative challenge and spirituality is siphoned out of the experience, becoming packaged into the sterile diagnostic codes of the DSM, profit-driven medicalization, etc.

The current landscape presents quite the corrosive synergy between these imbalance forces. However, it is good to keep in mind that one should approach homosexuality and transgenderism with a compassionate detachment, recognizing them as meaningful configurations of the soul, albeit ones that do not stand for norms. There is a challenge involved in these identities, since they can be considered soul-level conditions arising from the chosen tasks of the individual. The appropriate feeling should neither be endorsement nor condemnation, it should be a solemn recognition of a difficult spiritual curriculum being undertaken. For the transgender individual, it is the path of reconciliation. For the homosexual, it is the path of navigating polarity and attraction outside prescribed biological standard. One must respect the individual’s freedom and inner experience as a manifestation of their soul’s journey. Persecution, mockery, or coercion are grave spiritual harms that interfere not only with their karmic lessons, but with your own, and eat away at your own moral-objective being. The ideal is to provide a space of understanding which acknowledges the reality of these conditions. However, in the real world, this is much easier said than done. Concretely, these soul conditions are amplified and distorted by spiritual forces, and so, one must cultivate discernment and active moral resistance to these distortions (and hence not the abstract condition of homosexuality or transgenderism), while still upholding the core principle of compassion for the individual. One should feel not towards the person, but towards the conditions of cultural and systemic forces that encourage the exploitation and distortion of these authentic spiritual conditions. So a rejection of the Luciferic inflation involves the rejection of upholding personal identity as an absolutist ideology separate from the world and reality, and likewise a rejection of the Ahrimanic reduction involves a rejection of attitudes which sterilize and commercialize the spiritual condition into a medical or technical problem (technical problem also includes the technical problem of logistics in the commercialization of homosexuality and transgenderism). One should feel a resistance to the spirit that seeks to “solve” the dissonance through drugs and surgery, which in actuality embeds human identity within a system of materialistic control/profit. All in all, don’t hate the player, hate the game.

Post Script on Masturbation

As regards the topic of masturbation, a friend asked me my thoughts on this topic. Allow me to be lazy and cite myself in a response:

It’s just one sided sexual activity. Ontologically, Kant makes the argument that sex outside of a monogamous marriage leads to objectification. In the case of masturbation, the (to be) loved person is reduced to a mere object of appetite. The conduct of a well adjusted individual would run counter to this since implicit in the sex act is the immediate presence of another that’s acknowledged on the level of personhood. You wouldn’t have sex with an animal, or some other thing that can’t consent (if you’re well adjusted). People want to have sex with another that wants to have sex with them. Yet the structure of masturbation is just this- once this desire is fulfilled, they are to be discarded with no further consideration since they have served their purpose. This tends to eliminate any moral obligation towards the other person, as they are seen as nothing more than a thing to be used and disposed of. There is another aspect of objectification that Kant mentions, and it involves the folly of one-sided determination. As you are objectifying the “actor” or “actress” on screen, you are turning yourself into an object. You turn yourself into a mere tool to satiate appetite. This connects more to the Categorical Imperative (which if you think about it is just the Law of Non-Contradiction) stuff since to turn them into means for your own ends is to destroy the structure of you in terms of your ethical obligations, as well as destroying the social fabric connecting you to other people. Moreover, Kant says that masturbation is worse than the horror of killing yourself (a bit dramatic but founded) as it debases you below the status of a beast, involving both self-destruction and surrendering to animal instincts. Below an animal, since a beast cannot debase itself into its animalistic impulses, as it already exists within an animalistic state. Cosmologically, there are a few ways to look at it. The first is in agreement with the institutional sciences of today. Practice can have negative effects: it can reinforce maladaptive neural reward pathways, contributes to sexual dysfunction, distort expectations through pornography, or interfere with emotional regulation and/or intimate relationships. However, what the sciences of today (neurology, psychology, social sciences) will not do, or rather can’t do, is inform one on the spiritual/moral normativity of such an act. In order to do this, the unbiased teacher would firstly begin with an exposition on sexual energy- what it is, it’s telos (start, end, and developmental paths), and results based off actions one would take. This can take the form of describing Kundalini, Prana, Ojas, etc. from the Vedic tradition, or Vril, which is just another name for a kind of spiritual energy (originating from Edward Bulwer-Lytton’s novel, he is the guy who is known for the quote “It was a dark and stormy night” [purple prose]), and then go into what concretely masturbation does. For example, Stylianos Atteshlis describes the unintended creation of “negative elementals” by improper use of sexual energy. Franz Bardon warns that misused sexual energy can create entities in the astral/etheric plane (if these words mean nothing to you, just read it as “the domain beyond the physical containing the psychological, emotional, etc.”) that are harmful. In addition to the negative stances and identification of what masturbation leads to, there is the positive stance of nofap and other such movements. Those are all fine and provide various benefits. However, I’m not an unbiased teacher and in addition to affirming some if not all of these frameworks, and in an effort to not write a book in general chat, I will end off by saying it’s better to not do it and to direct your attention elsewhere as an alternative outlet.

Regarding prostitution, or what people call “sex work” in order to make it more acceptable, it should be opposed in every way, shape, and form. If one wants to be “promiscuous”, they are free to do so. In fact, historically, there is quite the variety of “sexual expression” throughout the different cultures. But the promotion of transaction-based sexual energy is a black magic ritual. To the extent that this attitude of promiscuity escapes the current era of market society, it may find its place as a kind of performance art.

On Sexual Energy Manipulation

As mentioned above, before the split in the ancient Lemurian epoch and prior, humanity existed in a unified androgynous spiritual form. Procreation was an immaculate process guided directly by the divine hierarchies (the Elohim, or the Spirit of Form, Jehovah, working through the Moon forces). The generative force existed, but not as we understand it. It was certainly not “sexual energy” in a polarized male-female sense. It existed as a pure, creative force flowing unconsciously through the unified human being used for virginal fructification, where the spirit would incarnate without physical intercourse. At this stage, there was no possibility or need for its manipulation by the human ego, or “I”. It was the division into sexes that liberated spiritual forces, which were previously used for bodily creation. This was the “price of thought”, as Steiner puts it. However, it also created a duality, and a descent into matter. The force became polarized and “corrupted”, seeing as the once unified creative force became bound to the newly formed material/physical male and female bodies. It descended into the “generative substances” and biochemical processes (hormones, the nervous system). It also became linked with desire via sensual pleasure and passion- look into Tree of Knowledge. This force was now in a bound, or fallen, state which served physical generation but trapped the spirit in matter. The conscious task of humanity is now to redeem this force, and return it to its higher, conscious purpose. Eventually we will transcend “manipulation”, but as of now, our task is in unifying this duality.

To clarify, this duality is not merely about behavior, it concerns the direction of consciousness and life force. There is the “downward path’ (the path of generation/dissipation) which has as it’s core principle outflow and externalization. Consciousness follows the force of desire outward, seeking union and satisfaction, and ultimately seeking creation in the external, physical world. The creative impulse which was born in the astral body as light and energy, is immediately channeled downward through the glandular and nervous systems. Becoming bound in the biochemical processes, it is expended in physical procreation, or the intense emotional charges of desire, passion, and personal attachment (so, including but not limited to masturbation, or other spiritual practices which we will get to shortly). As a result, the energy is “fixed” into matter, becoming a kind of animal sacrifice. The astral body is imprinted with passionate impressions (the sensory memories, attachments, and desires of these experiences) which creates a karmic content that must later be purified. While it serves the purpose of Earthly destiny and propels reincarnation, the other side of the bargain understands this as reinforcing identification with the physical self and thickens the veil separating us from the spiritual world. The symbol for this “downward path” is the Moon. It fits perfectly, in my opinion, since it reflects external light (the desire for outer objects), governs the cyclical biological rhythms of menstruation and fertility, and acts as the receptive principle (in many senses, but the most relevant sense is that it receives the sun). The “upward path”, or the path of regeneration/integration, on the other hand, has the opposite polarity/principle. It follows inflow and internalization and uses will to withdraw the force from external objects and redirect it inward, seeking union and creation within the internal, spiritual being. Through conscious resistance to immediate gratification (such as via the act of sacrifice), the same astral impulse is halted in its downward course. To put it another way, by “holding the seed” it is conserved. This is done through meditation, moral idealism, artistic creation, or various other techniques, the dammed up force is (to borrow a term from alchemy) sublimated. It is guided upward, and re-absorbed into the nervous system, resulting in a direction to nourish the brain and spiritual centers (or chakras). The energy here is redeemed, since it ceases to be generative (in the sense of procreating physical children) and becomes the procreative spiritual consciousness of the “Virgin Sophia”. Of course, the symbol here is the Sun. The sun emanates its own light and signifies the active, masculine principle. Both paths are perfectly depicted in the ancient emblem of the Caduceus of Hermes. The central staff is the human spine (sushumna), with the two intertwining serpents representing the downward, lunar, feminine, and upward, solar, masculine currents of force. Their crossing points are the spiritual centers, or chakras. The task of the initiate is to balance and unite these two “serpents” at each level, culminating in the wings at the crown- the liberation of the spirit.

The symbol of the centaur also provides us with a very potent archetypal image for the concepts of the upward and downward spiritual paths. It represents the dual nature within every person, with you as the centaur. The higher spiritual consciousness, or the human rider, is tasked with guiding and mastering the lower “astral beast”, or the animalistic realm of drives and passions and instincts which forms the lower part of your “soul” (the astral body). This inner struggle directly corresponds to the choice between the two paths of downward, following desires into physical expression, or the upward path of sacrifice and redirection of the same energy inward for spiritual regeneration. The reason this maps so well as a symbol is because it reiterates the actual stage of human development during the Lemurian epoch, where our ancestry were half animal and half spiritual beings. This was before the separation of the sexes, and the full emergence of physical sexuality in the middle of the Lemurian. Hence, the centaur is the primordial human condition from which the powerful source of sexual energy later differentiated and became something that required conscious redemption and mastery over.

Many traditions have latched onto this understanding of “generative”, or sexual energy. I will go over a few from certain traditions as a cross-cultural validation for the understanding provided above, for the reason that they all describe the same universal principles of life force polarity, and transformation, albeit using different cultural languages.

Prana is the universal vital force in Yoga, representing the omnipresent raw material. It is the “breath of life” which animates both the unified pre-split human, and the polarized being of today. Vril, from 19th century occultism, most closely resembles the operational force of regeneration as a tangible, and utilizable, etheric power that results from an inner alchemy. It functions as the battery for spiritual faculties. Because my website has the yin-yang symbol on it, I will go a bit more in depth on this aspect. The concept of Yin and Yang from Taoism furnishes for us the fundamental law that underlies spiritual reality, as the description of the interpenetrating process of transformation itself. Yin (receptive, lunar, contracting) and Yang (active, solar, expanding) are interdependent phases in one continuous cycle, each containing the seed of the other, perpetually giving way to one another like a cosmic breath (breath is used a lot, one can read “breath” in most contexts as “spirit”). The “downward path” is best understood as the phase where Yang turns toward Yin, as a description of active force condensing and becoming receptive to manifestation. The Upward path is the opposite, where Yin is transformed into Yang, through receptive consolidation and inner alchemy. The energy is purified and catalyzed to release its latent spiritual potential upward. This process of conscious transformation is the explicit focus of the Taoist internal alchemy known as Neidan, which seeks to unify both within the human body-microcosm, to generate the immortal “golden elixir”. This work operates through the three treasures of Jing, Qi, and Shen.

In order to avoid this becoming a Daoist metaphysics article, it is best to stop there. The spiritual “paths” described center on the transformation of the foundational forces found within human nature. Such work begins with the understanding that man is a merger of a higher spiritual part, and a lower, animalistic astral part which houses our drives and passions, including our sexuality. The core task should not be confused with a repression or suppression of these forces, since this kind of activity will eventually burst from within, in one way or another. Instead, one should seek the conscious transformation of sexual energy through the “I” or higher consciousness. As such, sexual energy in this view is a form through which deeper spiritual forces manifest.

Feminism

Modern “feminism’s” development is inextricably tied to the logic of capitalism/modernity. This alliance, has from the very onset, influenced the meaning of the liberation of woman, commodifying it into a product to be acquired. Freedom can no longer be framed as collective emancipation from “patriarchal” structures, instead being transformed into the individual’s capacity for increased participation in the labor market, and the expansion of personal consumerist choice. Of course, the aspirational icon is also transformed, becoming the “financially independent” woman whose power is stamped by her salary and purchasing ability. In this paradigm, the struggle shifts from eradicating oppression - and I mean true oppression - into securing a more equitable distribution of individuals within the existing hierarchy of the marketplace. Some may argue that feminism was not always this blatantly Mammonic, however I will address this soon. The primary critique of this form of feminism is understood to be that “oppression” against the fairer sex is not actually a goal. Instead, it is alignment with economic power and achieving a professional status within the established network of power. The cry for a “seat at the table” concedes the table’s existence and legitimizes it, focusing effort on a kind of inclusion within social institutions and civic society. The ultimate outcome is a version of feminism that creates the “girlboss” archetype; the ambitious, resilient, self-optimizing, perpetually productive worker. She is encouraged to bypass structural critiques in favor of John Henryist grind-culture, seeing her exhaustion as a badge of honor, or in some cases, using work as a therapeutic outlet for deeper problems we will soon discuss.

Modern feminism, being shaped by a capitalist/modernist framework, tacitly accepts “masculinity” as the dominant and legitimate mode of interfacing with the world. The values of this mode, abstract rationality, competitive ambition, compartmentalization, the will to dominate and transform external matters, and so on and so forth, are treated as the default standard for success and personhood, instead of one polarity of human potential. In order to be recognized as full participants in civil society, women are implicitly encouraged to adopt this attitude. What results is an assimilation where the worth of a woman’s contribution is measured by how adeptly she performs a masculinized script of disengagement and production. Likewise, the existential dimensions historically associated with womanhood are systematically erased or devalued. Roles like mother, grandmother, and daughter (with this last one having overlap with the section on transgenderism) which encompass relationships beyond biology, into continuity, sacrifice, etc. are relegated to the private sphere as a one among many “personal choice” instead of being honored as culturally and spiritually central vocations. Deeper feminine principles are dismissed as unproductive passivity or sentimentalism. The most tragic aspect of modernity’s erasure of womanhood is not that it elevates women into a true, integrated masculinity, but that it pressures them to perform a bastardized caricature of it. What is presented as “empowerment” is often the adoption of masculinity’s most external and transactional aspects, stripped of all the virtues which traditionally gave masculinity its purpose in the first place. It is unsustainable. This is also a negative aspect for men as well, since men are forced to “erase the feminine”, so to say, in emphasizing the one-sided aspects of masculinity. We can consider “traditional” masculinity (drawing upon an ideal development, in contrast to its current negative manifestation. I don’t mean to advocate for “traditionalism”) in its archetypal understanding, as oriented towards mastery as service. The will to power was channeled through sacrifice or stewardship. The knight or equivalent figure protected the weak, the craftsman honored his materials, for example. The drive becomes tempered by a discipline and connection to the world beyond the self. The “girlboss” model, by contrast, promotes a necrotic puppet of capitalist-compatible pseudo-masculinity. Seizing the instruments of masculine agency, it severs these from any connection to a higher ethic or end.

How does this make sense? How can one seek mastery of the world but end up turning the self into an object to be mastered? How can one embrace the aggression of masculinity, but abandon the responsibility that prompted and results from it? Well, let’s be more concrete and talk about personhood. It makes perfect sense when one considers that the logical endpoint of modernity as a society which reduces all value to an abstract market logic. The same logic which produced the logic of masculinity in the girlboss was the kernel present in the foundational feminism of early America, specifically paralleling the antiblackness within mainstream feminism. Feminism in all current forms (so no alternative “womanist” conceptions unfortuantely) is structurally and philosophically antagonistic to “Black” life, particularly the black male life, as a function of this inverted coopting of masculinity. The white, bourgeois subject’s liberation is conceptualized in opposition to, or through the symbolic use of, black subjugation. In effect, we can say that feminism is not incomplete, it is an active participation in the logic of anti-Blackness, which is tightly bound with the logic of capitalism (although not eternally designated to it).

The mechanism is mirrored. Where the “girlboss” seizes the instruments of masculine agency but abandoned the responsibility which justifies it, mainstream liberal feminism has historically seized the instruments of political agency and abandons the ethic of the brotherhood man that would elevate this struggle into a truly universal, and justified movement. Seeking liberation, but ending up reinforcing the hierarchy of antiblackness is done by accepting the premise of the system as the only field of struggle, and even further, becoming a participant in the active perpetuation of this system. Here are some examples:

Simone de Beauvoir in The Second Sex famously analogizes women’s oppression to that of the black person. However, this is a philosophical appropriation that extracts black suffering from its specific, terroristic reality. De Beauvoir uses the “black man” as a fungible abstract rhetorical figure to illuminate white women’s struggle for recognition, while showing no substantive concern for the actual anti-black violence, or the particularity of black existence. A theoretical cannibalism is at play wherein feminist theory, even in its most “radical” forms, establishes itself by consuming the image of black subjugation while remaining indifferent to black life. We may also consider Shulasmith Firestone. Her pervasive narrative of the Black Panther Party as inherently and uniquely sexist, was popularized and intellectually framed by the Jewish feminist. In The Dialectic of Sex, Firestone presented black male revolutionaries as mimicking the white patriarchal power they claimed to oppose, arguing that their intention was to simply dominate black women as a compensation for their societal subordination. Another example of non-solidarity. The redirected gaze shifted the focus from the anti-black violence of the state to an internal critique of black male character, a form of “victim blaming”, of which modern feminists ironically detest. This also helped cement the institutional template of an enduring archetype of the black male radical as a hypocritical patriarch, which would then be later uncritically digested by gender analysis. Using this “pathology” of black masculinity, we can trace other damning tropes into feminist origins. Rape, as a patriarchal weapon, or, the thesis that black men use rape as a tool to dominate black women within a patriarchal framework is credited to feminists Joyce Williams and Karen Holmes. The idea that the black male saw violence and sexual aggression as forms of compensation for a lack of manhood imposed by racism is traced to the work of criminologist Lynn A. Curtis. Theories born from this feminist gaze, rooted in criminological assumptions, were sanitized and institutionalized by being detached from their white authors and repackaged as objective “gender studies”. Perhaps the worst figure one can point to in this dynamic is found in Bell Hooks. Far from offering any sort of liberatory “Black” feminism, Hooks crafted a political ethic that ultimately served white patriarchy and reinforced anti-black male stereotypes. There are two, out of many, interconnected manipulations we will focus on. The first is the pathologization of black masculinity, and the prescription of interracial marriage as salvation.

Hooks’ central depiction of black men is a repackaging of aforementioned 19th-century racist ethnology and 1970s feminist dogma. We can trace her narrative directly to feminists like Firestone, Williams, and Karen Holmes. She popularized these white-authored theories within black discourse. Even the portrayal of pre-colonial African societies as brutally patriarchal by hooks is an uncritical repetition of racist ethnology from slavery apologists such as Dr. Samuel Cartwright, who painted African men as savage oppressors, to justify colonial “rescue” narratives. Naturally, hooks provides a pseudo-historical basis for viewing black masculinity as inherently pathological. This leads to our second manipulation- the marriage to white patriarchs as liberation. Having painted black men as irredeemably patriarchal threats; a more dangerous “enemy within” the black community, Hooks implicitly positions relationships with white men as a path to safety, validation, and freedom. This drastically overlooks a grotesque history. Anti-miscegenation laws were primarily enforced against black men, not against white men seeking black women. By reframing those same men as potential liberators in the present, the material threat shifts from a white male violence (which can be argued to be the structural violence of capital), to black male violence. Functionally, this is a grift which served to break black solidarity. It presents black nationalism and power movements as inherently sexist threats, while offering assimilation via marriage to the white ruling class as the normative feminist solution. This is not a threat to what can be called “white supremacy” in any way, it is an inversion from the inside out as a form of “managing” it, where black women are encouraged to seek individualistic rescue within a system, rather than grasp any higher social truth in order to combat it.

The pursuit of feminism’s pursuit of political legitimacy led it to forge alliances with both the carceral state and the security apparatus underpinning the American empire. The movement’s strategic shift from the late 20th century toward state-centric solutions culminated in the Violence Against Women Act, and the promotion of mandatory arrest and no-drop prosecution policies in domestic violence cases. The function of this was a consciously empowered police and legal prosecution base. This result was catastrophic for black communities, fueling the mass criminalization of black men, and with a bit of bitter irony, black women victims at high rates, as police often “misread” defensive actions or chaotic scenes as antagonistic. The feminist lobby accepted this “collateral damage” (although it did not harm the white feminist base) as the cost of doing political business with the state. The international front fared no better, presenting a parallel in the [alleged] documented relationships of feminist icons with the U.S. security state during the Cold War. It revealed a pattern of counterinsurgency within feminism, aimed at neutralizing radical threats and steering dissent toward liberal, American-friendly ends. The case of Gloria Steinem is the most direct. In the 50s and 60s, Steinem worked for the independent research service, a front organization funded by the CIA-backed Congress for Cultural Freedom. Her role was to guide non-communist student activism in Europe, steering the youth away from Marxist traditions, toward a more benign, western liberalism. While no evidence suggests Bell Hooks was a direct asset, her work fits intimately within the legacy of liberal co-opting. Her focus on individual psychology and “healing”, and of course personal relationships with patriarchy, is contextualized for us as a depoliticizes shift. Her, and similar frameworks, dovetailed the CIA’s broader cultural strategy to divert energy from anti-imperialist and anti-capitalist struggles (which were targeted for destruction by COINTELPRO and other such agencies). Angela Davis presents another figure. Unequivocally, she was a priority target of the FBI’s COINTELPRO program, and state repression due to her politics and association with the BPP. Her later career, however, can be characterized as an iconic example of assimilation. The shift from calling for the abolition of the prison-industrial complex, to a tenured professor and feminist icon embraced by mainstream institutions, reflects a neutralization one must undergo in order to be integrated into the mainstream in the first place. It must also be said that Angela Davis supported Jim Jones of Jonestown infamy. Was she caught by the charismatic allure of Jones? Or was supporting him one of the conditions under which she would be integrated into the status quo? We may never know.

A question one might have had rise up within them is, “why should I care about the black struggle being talked about with respect to feminism? How does this relate to me?”. The black struggle is not a peripheral issue of social justice, it exposes the core of modernity’s spiritual crisis as alliance with a system that severs human qualities from their purpose. Such a severance is built on a more primary severance of antiblackness, which is the most prominent concretized form of the abstract severance of man from his purpose. All people today, are in a sense, although not to the same degree, defined as objects in order to enable a world of transactional individualism. The “girlboss” mastering herself and the feminist theorist using black suffering as a metaphor are symptoms of the same problem. Consequently, the black struggle is the struggle against the primary act of objectification upon which this fractured modern self is built, from grasping a new subjectivity, to finding this subjectivity reflected in the institutions. To ignore it, is to ignore the root of the malaise I describe, the problem everyone faces. Humanity’s task is a conscious integration. This task is impossible while the collective psyche is built on denying the full humanity of a part of itself.

So where does this lead “feminism” exactly? There is in a sense, an inevitability to a kind of “feminism”, particularly in this age. The establishment of the universal rational subject, or even more broadly, the “I”, would have eventually expanded to include all people, including women. This perspective gains a mighty force alongside a paradigmatic shift in technology and economics. When a society’s primary productive force ceases to be brute physical strength (a biologically dimorphic advantage) and becomes organized intelligence and technical skill, the material ground for patriarchy begins to dissolve. However, the materialist explanation only describes the mechanism of change in the physical world, not its ultimate cause. In order to grasp the possibility of a feminism, one must look to the spiritual or towards the ideal, and understand such historical developments, such as Enlightenment ethos, or the Industrial Revolution, which implies the shift to intellectual labor, as themselves the external manifestations of a deeper, internal event. This event is the full incarnation and awakening of the human “I”, or ego. This awakening spirit demands social forms that allow it to exercise its freedom, and henceforth generates the philosophies and technologies that clear the physical stage for its emergence. The material erosion of gendered roles certainly cannot be the cause of liberation. It must be understood as an effect of a spiritual imperative. True women’s libration, and men’s liberation from the one-sided masculinity of this current epoch by extension, exists as a spiritual possibility where the genderless “I”, incarnated in a male or female body, consciously integrates and spiritualizes both polar principles in itself, and is allowed to freely accomplish its goals without the projection of external determination and subjugation.

I personally used to think that masculinity and femininity were differently manifested in men and women, based on the gender of the practitioner. But if this is true, then it locks women into gender-locked roles, tied down by sex. We remain confined in a logic where a woman’s ambition is forever “feminine ambition”, and her strength is a “feminine strength”, always differing in kind and purpose from a man’s. Her potential is filtered through a gendered lens, her highest aspirations subtly redirected toward roles considered appropriate for her “nature”. Likewise, man’s vulnerability is seen essentially as weakness. What would it mean for the active principle of masculinity to be vulnerable? It goes against its very definition (of course, as conceived one-sidedly). Not only does this view change when one considers the true character of masculine and feminine, it also changes when we consider the soul’s journey across lifetimes. If the spirit incarnates alternately as man and woman (as many traditions, as well as Steiner suggests, and of course there are exceptions here), then these categories of “male experience” and “female experience” at best are not the final destinations, and at worst are incoherent. The soul does this to gather all possible human experiences; to know the world from within the polarity of giving and receiving, acting and enduring, speaking and listening, pushing and pulling, and so on and so forth, in all their physical and social configurations. A soul may cultivate a formidable will in one life as a man, and in the next as a woman, learn to wield this will through patience and inner resilience. The point is not to perfect masculinity or femininity, but to master the full spectrum of human capacity, which transcends both.

From this perspective, a “real” feminism becomes possible. One informed by the conscious understanding of human development. This feminism would not fight for women to merely occupy men’s roles, as the “girlboss” does. Instead, it would work to create a society where the individual’s spiritual task of integration is supported by the outer world. Practically, this means freedom for every person, in whatever body they happen to inhabit. This comes in the form of advocating for social and economic structures that allow for the full expression of human variety. Women do face legitimate problems, which we may consider as a neglect. A neglect of both women, sure, but also of the “feminine” mode of interfacing with the world, of which this current epoch is biased against. So if a “feminism” emerges which is informed by an anthroposophical outlook, and covers the practical side of menstruation/toiletry products, public infrastructure, healthcare, and the like, there should be no problem in providing it support.

item.title: Pragmatism and the Precept: Focus on the Concrete Through William James
item.description:
In short, the notion that real contingency and ambiguity may be features of the real world is a perfectly unimpeachable hypothesis. Only in such a world can moral judgments have a claim to be.
—William James, On Hegelisms

Intro

William James lived during a time of upheaval. A confluence of forces found in the Civil War, the rise of industrialism, a challenge of Darwinianism to old forms of seemingly dogmatic faith, and waves of new immigration situated him at the crossroads of science, religion, and philosophy. This portrayed him as a therapeutic thinker whose central mission was to make sense of these competing worldviews that troubled individual minds and the culture at large. He was, after all, America’s most influential philosopher at one point in time. Reflected in his thought is an American experience; his leading significance is in a “pragmatism” best fit for a young, pluralistic, action-orientated demos. James’s pragmatism offered a faith which rejected the absolute pessimism or optimism offered previously in favor of the belief that the world is changeable for the better through human effort. Specifically, that in the face of totalizing worldviews, the actual existence of evil and ambiguity is an integral part of what makes faith significant in the first place. He also provided a philosophy which opposed rigid, “monolithic” systems of thought. Embracing a “pluralistic universe” where multiple perspectives could coexist (as long as they cashed out in practical benefits), this mirrored the American ideal (if not always the practice) of integrating diverse peoples and ideas into a New Atlantis. Conceived of this way, James can be said to have been a key architect of a distinctively American culture of thought. He championed an intellectual style which was accessible, grounded in the concrete modes of life, and deliberately spoke to a broad public sociality. Philosophy for him was framed as a vital set of tools for addressing life’s concrete problems, as opposed to a technical puzzle for specialists, or in other words, philosophy is “what works” for individuals and society in navigating a world which they face. His ideas were what in part became embedded in America’s conscious self-conception, from progressive education, to the experimental ethos of the New Deal, to literature. All in all, he is the defining philosopher of American spirit during America’s transition to modernity. Therefore, James’s pragmatism is not to be understood as just a theory of truth, but as a therapeutic tool for a particular America in the past which opposed any perceived imposition - be it dogmatic religion, scientific materialism, or the Absolute Idealism of Hegel - that neglected the concrete, immediate ‘precepts’ of being, in favor of abstract, totalizing explanations. His entire philosophy can be seen as a project to rescue and vindicate the “given” percept.

The mission to rescue the precept surely wasn’t an abstract academic choice for James (as it would be contrary to his philosophy of pragmatism), it was an outgrowth of a personal history encountered in the neglected given itself. Two foundational experiences were what compelled his project to take the shape that it did. The first is a Swedenborgian upbringing that treated spiritual reality as a concrete fact. The second is a mystical encounter under nitrous oxide that delivered an overwhelming “tremendously exciting sense of an intense metaphysical illumination” where “every opposition… vanished in a higher unity” and “the ego and its objects… are one”. Considering he was the son of a devout follower of Emanuel Swedenborg, Henry James Sr., familial inheritance can also be included in the former. Swedenborg presented his visions as detailed empirical reports; a descriptive science of the afterlife, angelic societies, and the correspondence between spiritual and natural laws. Growing up in this environment, it lends one to treat spiritual reality as a concrete dimension of experience to be investigated and described, and the attitude which incites an aversion to systems that would dismiss certain phenomena as an illusion. Thus, his later philosophical mission to expand empiricism to its radical limits, embracing all that is experienced, can be seen as a secularization and philosophic grounding of a Swedenborgian sensibility absorbed in youth.

The personal experimentation with nitrous oxide inspired an illumination in him, not unlike those who, in modern day, who take psychedelics and come away with some or other profound realization of the world. The most fundamental oppositions of subject and object, self and the world, and thinking and being dissolved in a “higher unity” as a direct overwhelming precept of unity- a raw, undeniable feeling of coalescence that was beamed right into his consciousness. For a committed empiricist and critic of Hegelianism, this was a disorientating event. It presented a brute fact of experience that his existing intellectual systems could not explain away. Such an experience became the test case for his emerging philosophy in asking, “how could thought do justice to such a powerful, noetic, yet at the same time transient and ineffable moment of experienced being?”. His entire project in The Varieties of Religious Experience and the development of Radical Empiricism can be read as an attempt to build a philosophical system which can accommodate this kind of pre and post [Hegelian] conceptual revelation without reducing it in some way to either pathology, some lower order of something purely logical, or dismissing it outright as nonsense.

His commitment to the concrete precept led James into the dimly lit parlors of parapsychological research. Active participation in seances and his foundational role in establishing the American Society for Psychical Research are the rational extension of his radical empiricism, despite what some might claim. For James, reports of telepathy, clairvoyance, mediumship, etc. were empirical claims about the human experience which demanded rigorous investigation. Even famously studying the medium Leonora Piper for years, becoming convinced that certain phenomena, particularly telepathy, pointed to genuine, if poorly understood, connections between minds that transcended the ordinary channels of sense, he went as far as to claim that the future will corroborate the existence of telepathy. This work exemplifies a core philosophical courage in following the data of experience wherever it leads, even to the fringes of accepted science, and to defend the integrity of the precept.

Note: Another famous figure associated with therapy also did research on telepathy: Sigmund Freud.

Primacy

Having established James’s life and experience and briefly giving an overview of his pragmatism, we can now begin to describe the novelty of his view. Often grounded under the banner of “pragmatism”, James’s system (which is more accurately termed Radical Empiricism) diverges from the “pragmatism” of his peers. For Charles Sanders Peirce, the founder of pragmatism, or pragmaticism, as he later called it, the meaning of a concept lay in the conceivable practical effects of its object, a rule for clarifying thought and inquiry within a community of scientists. The focus of Peirce was in formal logic and semiotics, depriving truth of its grounding and placing it onto the opinion of an infinite scientific community. Later, positivistic interpretations would reduce pragmatism to the embarrassing “verification principle”, where “what works” is synonymous with “what is empirically measurable”. However, for James, pragmatism was never simply a theory of meaning or a handmaiden to laboratory science. His pragmatism was the servant of the full stream of immediate experience. The “practical” and “what works” is judged in the far more expansive criterion of a belief or concept being validated by its ability to do justice to and enrich the content put forth to consciousness. Does a belief in free will, in the spiritual significance of a mystical event, or in the moral texture of the universe help an individual connect more vitally, act more courageously, and make richer sense of the palpable, “jolting” quality of life? If so, it has pragmatic truth. Consequently, where other pragmatisms might dismiss religious or moral experiences as subjective epiphenomena, James’s radical empiricism demanded that they be included as constitutive parts of the world to be explained. At core, James’s philosophy of pragmatism is the defense of the dignity and ontological weight of the precept, known as raw and indeterminate observed being, against any system that would reduce or exclude it. This commitment finds its completion in his critique of Hegelianism (or rather the Hegelians of his day). James readily acknowledged the shared philosophical desire to show that the ideal is real, his objection was to the methodological violence perceived in Hegel’s system. The actual delivery of reality to consciousness is not a smooth, logical emanation, but something far more primal and disruptive. “The parts seem, as Hegel has said, to be shot out of a pistol at us”, James writes. “Arbitrary, foreign, jolting, discontinuous—are the adjectives by which we are tempted to describe it.” This “jolt” is the hallmark of the pure precept, the unmediated givenness of things before the intellect goes to work. Hegelianism, for James, in achieving its elegant and totalizing rationality, must “butcher” a great part of the world’s contents, forcibly relegating the qualitative richness of immediate experience to the dust-bin labelled Maya, only to be subjugated to the Hegelian Concept.

This is the crux of James’s departure from what he would term “monolithic” idealism. He does not deny the human drive for unity or ideal comprehension, instead insisting that such unity must be discovered within the concrete floe of relations between percepts, rather than being imposed upon them by a logical schema. The ideal for him is the connective tissue found in the “great continua” of space, time, and personal consciousness that provide a “common table” for the world’s diverse elements. This leads to his famous pluralistic metaphor: The universe is not an Absolute, but a “Republican Banquet” where the myriad “qualities of being respect one another’s personal sacredness” while partaking in shared relations. In this model, the precept retains its “sacred” integrity. James’s pragmatism then is the tool to navigate a precept-laden world, making it work for man. It is a pragmatism where truth successfully connects one percept to another within the spectacle of experience.

The Precept’s Destiny

James’s critique of Hegelianism correctly identifies the violence of a philosophy that, in its drive for total thinking immanence and coherence, must dissolve the immediate, indeterminate data of consciousness as a moment of logical system. In defending this realm, James performs the invaluable service of forcing philosophy to contend with the primacy of the percept, the unmediated encounter that is the necessary starting point for all knowledge, and what all systems of knowledge must confront. Rightly protesting against a rationalism that would dismiss the concrete, qualitative being of the world, as an overcoming by some higher logic or principle, his rebellion is justified and essential.

However, for all its rebellious power, this pragmatism inverts the Hegelian “neglect” and fails to provide an account of the very precept it seeks to defend by asserting the primacy of the precept over the concept. By situating the percept’s validity within a pluralistic psychological economy of what “works” for the individual, he inadvertently reduces it. In his system, the percept is a terminal point; it is a brute and opaque fact whose meaning is determined by its subsequent utility in the stream of subjective experience. While its reality is confirmed by moral and practical value, its intrinsic ontological significance remains less than unexplored. It is externally-determined and unfree. The flaw of this perspective sees that precept and concept are not two independent entities, one is primary, and one is secondary. They are inseparable poles of a unified generative/creative act. The concept is not applied to the precept from the outside. It is the active inward force through which the percept’s latent meaning is awakened and realized. Green is not intelligible as “foliage” because of a practical association, it becomes intelligible because the spiritual activity of thinking actively brings the idea of the plant into relationship with the sensory impression; in the act of thought, concepts are arrived at and realized as the unifying principle which brings coherence to a manifold of sense impression. Therefore, James is right that Hegel’s system, in its majestic sweep, can trample the particularity of the precept. But he is wrong to believe the precept can stand alone as self-sufficient. By making the precept primary, he leaves it fundamentally unintelligible on its own terms. He defends its existence, but at the same time severs it from the very power that gives it meaning.

On a final note, the defining truth of William James lies in his unshakeable commitment to an expansive reality. James’s radical empiricism performs the essential work of shattering the idealist philosopher’s veto on what constitutes legitimate experience. By insisting that the full spectrum of the “given” - including but not limited to moral urgency, telepathy, and sense perception - he establishes the percept in all its varieties as philosophically inviolable. There is a transformation of the immediate, indeterminate being of the world brought to consciousness from a problem to be solved, into a mandate for any true ontology to actually confront and connect with on unbiased terms. This radical empiricism finds a strong alliance with the precept of Steiner, who asserts that the immediate presentation of the world, whether it be in a leaf, or a thought, is not an illusion to be explained away by the mineralistic and natural sciences, but the content of which is the starting point of true knowledge of things. The concrete, then, is not physical. It is the total, rich content of encounter, before it is infringed upon by theory. It is quite admirable that the pragmatic insistence on the concrete is not entirely wrong to the extent that it honors the sovereignty of the “moment of truth”. What any given thing is supposed to do is found in its concrete being-in-the-world. Any declared intention or pre-existing theory is washed away by a radical fidelity to the observable fact of the matter, and in fact this “fact” is proof of the concept in question. Likewise, the precept is the output of a deeper ideality or system, and in order to understand that system (whether it is the thinking of Nature, or the thinking of Spirit), an indispensability of the output must be established. The defense of such a moment takes the form of a foundational element of the American philosophic character; William James is the pragmatic spirit insistent on the authority of the concrete, and oriented toward truths borne of action and proven on their own merits. Therefore, the significance to be extracted from James is in the imperative which seeks comprehension by first humbling oneself towards what is given, for only then does one earn the right to speak on the truth of a matter.

Header: The Gross Clinic, Thomas Eakins (1875)

item.title: The Development of Human Physiology and Spirituality
item.description:
Note: This is a first draft of this article, probably [not] to be updated in the future. As a result, some of the writing is not up to par, repetitive, unclear. For now, it is informative enough to post.

Intro

Human development is among the first order concerns of humanity. After all, self-knowledge is the beginning of all true knowledge, regardless of if this self is a broad and abstract notion of spirit/source, or if this “self” is the immediate ground by which the possibilities for the conditions of freedom may realize themselves. As it follows then, a brief and high level elementary note on what “man”, or “humanity”, is constituted by is necessary. Man is ultimately a category of being which is capable of self-determination. As a consequence, we are defined much less by what we are than what we can become. It’s quite a paradoxical position we find ourselves in, since in order to realize freedom and determine ourselves, certain conditions need to be in place. Namely, the possibility of failure, non-being or a gradual falling-away from the kind of category we are (which comes hand in hand with failure. It is entirely possible to do deeds which would eventually have us ceasing to be human), and most importantly, a concretization of this being in the world. These conditions demonstrate for us the fact that freedom is not given, just as the saying goes: you can lead a horse to water, but you cannot make it drink. Just as the horse needs to want the water in order for it to drink, freedom needs to be realized by the soul of the individual as a free act wholeheartedly, otherwise it is not freedom.

The capacity for freedom does not appear fully formed. In the aforementioned conditions, the unfolding of this category of being through time, such as organic, mechanic, chemical, and spiritual structures, are of key importance to actualize the decisions man makes. The precise workings and detailing of this process will occupy us subsequently, but first we must establish the sobering fact that the complex organism “man” has many perspectives by which we may view ourselves. Body, soul, and spirit are all interrelated, and likewise the temptation must be resisted to view ourselves through a singular lens. The life and development of each and every person is contrapuntal; each narrative framing of an individual is equally true as the next, forming a totality. The same can be said for the abstract sense of development to be described. Further, the human being reveals itself only through multiple, overlapping perspectives. It is through this framework that we can begin to comprehend ourselves.

Were it not for the need to make itself real in a specific kind of way, the human being would never have achieved the conditions of freedom. In other words, if the human being were to remain purely spiritual, the exposure to external impulses and influences that sway them from a realized self-determination, and likewise freedom would not be realized. As a result, the human needs an environment to both give them the opportunity to fail (be swayed by externality), and provide some kind of conditionality which acclimates it to this externality while preserving the possibility for freedom. It is necessary that the human being is posited into nature and fashions itself a body with help from the higher spirits. More will be said in another article on this topic.

Threefold Man (Or is it four?)

Throughout various cultures and history, the differing perspectives on the totality of man have a consistent through line. Each schema emphasizes a different dimension of the same human being. For instance, the trichotomy of body, soul, and spirit is the most ancient and widespread. It makes note of the difference of the physical side, the inner life of feeling and consciousness (soul), and the eternal, self‑conscious ideality (spirit). This is the broadest division. In Plato’s Phaedrus, the soul is depicted as a charioteer driving two horses, a division that echoes into Neoplatonism and early Christian theology, where Paul’s distinction of “spirit, soul, and body” became the foundation for later trichotomist thought. When Steiner speaks of a threefold functional system (to be clarified later), he is extending this old intuition into a physiological and spiritual scientific language, carefully distinguishing the living organism from the metaphysical. The fourfold enumeration finds its classic expression in the Pythagorean Tetractys. For those unfamiliar, the Tetractys is the sacred decad (meaning 10. It is 10 because 10 contains within it the enumerations of 4: 4 + 3 + 2 + 1 = 10) arranged as a triangle of ten points, symbolizing the four elements, the fourfold structure of number, and the cosmos itself. Much can be said on the Tetractys. In Jewish Kabbalah, the four worlds (Atziluth, Beriah, Yetzirah, Assiah) describe a layered descent from spirit to matter, analogous to the fourfold human constitution of physical, etheric, astral, and “I.” Steiner explicitly references the Tetractys as a prefiguring of his own fourfold model. Sevenfold schemas permeate world traditions. We have the seven chakras of yogic philosophy, the seven metals of alchemy, the seven days of creation in Genesis, and the seven planets of antiquity all point to a structuring of the cosmos and the human being into seven interrelated and interpenetrating levels, to name a few. Ninefold enumerations appear most notably in the Christian angelic hierarchies, for instance where Dionysius the Areopagite arranged the nine choirs of angels into three triads. Similarly, the ninefold Enneagram, though modern, draws on ancient roots of threefold and sevenfold patterns. For Steiner, the ninefold view emerges when we distinguish the soul’s three distinct activities, each of which can be progressively transformed by the “I.” Each of these numbers - whether three, four, seven, nine - represents a different lens through which the various spiritual traditions of man have glimpsed the same object. I’d hate to sound like a broken record, but much more can be said on this, and on the totality of this development. Among these lenses, one of the more comprehensive ways of understanding the living human being is as so: firstly a description of the more functional natural distinctions which condition freedom, then how exactly they encourage a development of the threefold soul and I, and finally, a few notes on what I call the “rhythms of life”. This will present us with a schema that unveils how the human being expresses thinking, feeling, and willing in a much more concrete way than the enumerations above. Please keep in mind that this is not comprehensive, as it does not take into account things like the relationship man has to these components during incarnated life vs. the time from death and new birth, the influence of the hierarchies, zodiacal influence, and so on and so forth.

Threefold Being

The three functional subsystems of nervous, rhythm, and metabolic-limb pervade the whole organization of man. Through each system’s “center of gravity” so to say, each encourages a relationship to consciousness and spiritual influence. Firstly is the head organization. The nerve-sense subsystem has its center of gravity in the “head” and is the most spiritualized of the three systems because, ironically, it is the most physical and the one most subject to death. It is the oldest evolutionary inheritance, formed during Old Saturn, Sun, and Moon stages, and is therefore the “dying” part of the human organism. It’s physical foundation is the nervous system, which Steiner describes as the “lyre of Apollo”- an instrument through which cosmic harmonies are translated into individual soul experience. Furthermore, it is also the bearer of thinking and conceptual life. The distinctive feature is an operation through death. What this means is that the brain requires a continuous process of mineral deposition and subtle decay to serve as the instrument of thought. The nervous system constantly “dies” in order to create the space for spirit to enter.
