The Cartesian cogito establishes only an abstract, empty identity between thought and being, which fails to encompass any real content. This creates an irreducible dualism between the thinking substance and extended matter. Ironically, by defining matter purely through the intellectual property of extension, Descartes’ “materialism” ends up portraying matter as a rarefied product of thought, thereby spiritualizing it. As for Kant, I have written in depth about the transition from Kant to Hegel here. While not exactly being the culmination of Greek thought, Hegel’s project was the ending of the modern mind’s struggle in overcoming isolation. In this way, the solution that is Hegel’s system is instead a symptom of the very crisis it’s trying to solve. Absolutely, it is worthy of study, if not for historical reasons, then because of the strength of thought it develops in oneself if taking his project seriously. But as the proposed ultimate intelligibility of the world, it is in reality the most sophisticated and developed expression of the problem, masquerading as its solution. The drive to unify everything into a single rational structure is the ultimate expression of the modern, sovereign “I”, asserting its intellectual dominion over all reality. This is the philosophical equivalent of the Scientific Revolution’s mission statement of mastering nature through reason, just applied to history and Ideality. His system is a staggeringly intricate, and at times beautiful, map of a territory, trying to convince us that the map is the territory. In being the expression of the isolated modern intellect, it tries to generate cosmic meaning from its own logical operations after failing to find the necessity/intelligibility in the given myths and stories of the previous age (what he would call truth in representational form, or religion, which bears genuine content, but also of which the thinking subject cannot accept as valid since its necessity is not produced immanently by reason, thereby leaving the subject-object divided). Likewise, the meaning produced from the system, for example the concept of life, is a shadow over the living reality of life, rather than directly receiving the meaning from the object in question, while grasping it internally as necessary and true. As a result, all claims from philosophy, with Hegel’s system of Absolute Idealism in mind, about the world end up being coincidental. Insofar as the structure of reason is true, there is a direct correlate in the world. But this does not equate to truly unifying the subject and object.

Having pushed the qualitative project of this sovereign “I” to its absolute limit, Hegel’s system did not resolve modern alienation. Almost immediately, the reaction to his system was followed by attitudes that dismantled the qualitative dimension. In the wake of Hegel, two attitudes emerged that cleared the ground for the quantification of totality.

Materialism

The first was the objectification of the source, or subject. Thinkers like Ludwig Feuerbach and the young Karl Marx held that the “I” is no longer the source of meaning (Spirit) in thought, but rather a node within a larger web. Feuerbach’s claim is that Hegel’s Spirit is a projected human essence. The “I” is not the vehicle of cosmic reason, it is a being which is natural and sensuous. This reaction dragged the “I” down from the realm of pure thought into the sensuous world of biology and psychology - into the world of empirical study. Marx similarly said the “I” is not defined by consciousness, but by social relations and material praxis. The thinking subject is a product of economic and historical forces, in effect, making the “I” itself an effect within an analyzable system of objects. It is important to recognize that the “materialist inversion” is the shadow of the Cartesian moment. Where Descartes’ isolated cogito declared: “I think, therefore I am” and secured the thinking self as the only certainty, the “materialist inversion” proclaims: “I labor, therefore we transform”. It actually accepts the cogito’s declaration of independence from a mythic cosmos, but reject’s its loneliness. It claims that the “I” is not a singular existence, but is in fact a node in a collective, laboring human species-being. This inversion doesn’t solve the subject-object split, instead it radicalizes the Cartesian instance of domination.

As Slavoj Žižek observes in the Soviet ethos, the materialist project becomes “man’s domination over nature” (I could be misattributing this to him, although I think it’s fair to characterize the Soviet ethos this way). The Cartesian “I,” which sought to master nature (or the world) through mathematics, is collectivized into a historical “we” that seeks to master nature through industry and science through the social substance known as the Soviet people. In shifting the goal from individual certainty to collective control, intelligibility is no longer about finding meaning in the world, but about extracting utility from it. The qualitative “I” that contemplated and participated is replaced by the quantitative “human factor” that must be optimized and engineered. The “materialist inversion” completes the severance initiated by Descartes. It takes the sovereign subject and embeds it within a material system that the subject is meant to dominate, turning the gaze of analysis outward and downward onto the very conditions of its own existence. A dualism is at play here. The “I” becomes both the agent of domination, and a unit within the dominable field. This duality is exactly what makes the “I” ripe for measurement. To dominate nature efficiently, you must first calibrate the instrument.

The materialist inversion, while some claim it to be radical, did not necessarily lead to the positivist world that gave birth to the modern concept of intelligence. By embedding the “I” within a historical struggle or sensuous human essence, it preserved the qualitative dimension, or a form of telos for man. Whether it be the liberation of the species, or the realization of man’s potential. Intelligence here is still intelligible as praxis, taking the form of an engagement with the world inseparable from the enabling conditions which the “I” actualizes itself through. Meaning, the exclusive now-ness of will, community, and historical context. One could not isolate and score this on a sheet, since its value was in its very application toward collective and social ends. It can be rightly said then, that this materialist inversion presaged intelligence becoming a commodity of force in the world. It presaged the Ahrimanic impulse, the drive toward absolute immanence, materialism, and mechanistic dominion over the world. However, the materialist inversion was an impulse still tempered, no matter how faintly, by a humanistic purpose. And so the “I” was an object of science, but in the service of the social substance, and as a result, a grand transformation.

Positivism

The qualities of the “I” can now be studied as objects. This is an important precondition for measurement. The second reaction to Hegel was the positivist one. Rather than objectifying the “I” from the outside, there is a dissolving involved of its core qualitative experience as a legitimate category of knowledge. The reaction is essentially: “Hegel wasn’t wrong, just meaningless!”. Positivists claim his project is a result of misusing language, or a product of metaphysical confusion. This attitude is also home to the attitude of thinkers who assume that because they cannot understand something, their lack of understanding constitutes the unintelligibility of that thing. The very fact that something is makes it intelligible. Nevertheless, according to the positivist, statements about “Spirit”, “the meaning of history”, and even “subjective truth” are completely nonsensical. Early Wittgenstein, in his Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus, didn’t argue with Hegel, the action taken was drawing a boundary limit. Propositions could only picture states of affairs in the world. Everything else, everything ideal, like ethics, aesthetics, the meaning of life, and the nature of the “I” was unspeakable. It belongs to a category known as mystical. Sure, it could be shown how one lived, but it could not be said in any logically meaningful proposition and likewise communicated intelligibly. It’s not quite a critique, more like a juvenile rejection of accepting that the domain of qualitative subjective experience is a field of legitimate knowledge. Hence there can be no private language, according to early Wittgenstein. Bertrand Russell and the Vienna Circle turned this into a ruthless program known as the “verifiability principle”. A statement under this program could only be meaningful to a subject only if it is either analytic (true by definition, such as logic or math), or empirically verifiable. It was touted as a guillotine for concepts. Under this guillotine, the head of Hegel’s Spirit rolls, as do all concepts like “the good”, the “beautiful”, and paradoxically, the “I”. Supposedly.

This principle of “verificationism” is perhaps the ultimate expression of vapidity. Ignoring historical and logical preconditions, it fails its own test. If only analytic or empirically verifiable statements are meaningful, then it itself, being neither analytic nor empirically verifiable, stands as a decree about the nature of meaning which lacks meaning altogether on its own account. In fact, rather than refuting Hegel, it embodies the very same alienation that his philosophy aimed to overcome. Reason in this attitude becomes so terrified of reality, ambiguity, and the world, that it confines itself to a self-made cell of tautologies and instrument-readings, and call this the entire world. No, it doesn’t prove that qualitative experience is unreal. All it proves is that it is incapable of making it intelligible. After all, near the end of his life, AJ Ayer recognized this very critical blow, and admitted that he wasted his life on the movement, below @33:54:

The Modern Understanding of Intelligence

What remained after the positivist reaction to the situation the “I” found itself in, after declaring the qualitative dimension of the “I” unspeakable, was a ghost. The living, meaning-seeking subject is gone, and what remained in its place was the “I” as a logical-grammatical placeholder in the level of ideality. The “I” is only a bundle of sense data and set of behavioral dispositions. Intelligence, stripped of meaning and participation, was redefined as the sterilized functional capacity for valid logical manipulation and efficient problem-solving. No longer was it a faculty for rendering the world intelligible, but a kind of processing engine which navigates rule-bound systems. The operative definition of intelligence is now “abstracted, pure cognitive processing power”. Now it is not the soul’s capacity to render a meaningful cosmos intelligible, nor is it the mind’s participation in something intelligible. Stripped of all content, this redefinition is the functional efficiency of a biological logic-machine. “Solve for x, complete this series, manipulate these abstract relations”. This intelligence is computational and decontextualized. The value lies in generating correct outputs from defined inputs.

This conceptual vacuum created an irresistible demand. If intelligence is a generic processing power, then it must be measurable and rank-able. The bureaucratic and scientific mindset, fathered by a positivistic spirit, simply can’t sit aside and let a variable called intelligence remain a qualitative mystery, and so, an abstract model requires a concrete metric. This metric arrives in the form of the IQ test as an instrument to fulfil the modern understanding of intelligence. It is the applied, institutionalized truth of logical positivism. The test design perfectly mirrors the definition. The only skills exclusively measured are analytical skill and empirical pattern recognition, which are the very same competencies the positivist “I” was said to possess. Morality, out-of-the-box creativity (such as test takers. What if you are smarter than the test makers, can a test accurately capture this?), embodied wisdom, and more are completely left to the wayside. An assessment is not made of how one reads between the self and the world, all we have left is a standardized performance review of a supposed isolated cognitive processor.

The test’s history certainly confirms its role as an applied ideology. Alfred Binet developed early scales as a practical, via negativa tool to identify educational need. However, it was successors like Lewis Terman in America, infused with eugenic ideology, and positivist spirit, who transformed it into the via positiva metric we recognize today. Such successors operationalized the abstract notion of general processing power (g) into a single score, claiming to represent innate, hierarchically distributed intellectual worth. In doing so, the foundational error of the test was established: the bankruptcy of conceptual value. With its single-scored nature creating a self-justifying loop, intelligence was defined as what the test measures, and the test was hailed as the measure of intelligence. The circular logic on display was grafted onto a statistical fallacy, treating the mind’s complexity as a linear trait distributable along a bell curve. The resulting test became the key tool for rationalizing educational tracking, immigration policy, social triage, social hierarchy, and so on and so forth, measuring little more than the proxy skills of test-taking conformity and abstract puzzle solving. These are the very aptitudes of the bureaucrat and clerk, not the visionary or the sage. Thus, the modern IQ test is the bureaucratic apotheosis of the eviscerated modern “I”. It is the ultimate artifact of a consciousness that, having lost the world and then annihilated its own qualitative interior, could conceive of nothing more meaningful than to assign itself a number. What we have before us, is a quantitative monument built upon the buried grave of qualitative intelligence. In this, it stands as the perfect, pathetic shrine of our age.

Truly, it can be said that the real IQ test, the test which measures for intelligence, for how well one grasps intelligibility, is seeing through IQ test itself. To grasp it as a ritual for the modern age that reduces everything into Ahriman, into breakdown and quantification, is to pass the exam it is administering. In grasping that its numbers and patterns are a self-referential game, one demonstrates the very faculty the test cannot measure but is claiming to measure: the ability to read between the lines of a system or thing and discern its rules and non-immediate functions. The test rewards those who play by its rules. It satisfies the ego, and whole social environments are built upon it’s connotations. But it silently fails those who understand its irrelevance. The ultimate act of intelligence in the face of IQ is to perceive the boundaries of the instrument meant to contain intelligence.

Ahrimanic Gallery

The nerve-sense system is the bodily correlate of abstract, calculative thinking. Ahriman (depicted on the top right as a sculpture done by Rudolf Steiner) works by hypertrophying intellect while severing it from feeling and will. The enlarged head symbolizes consciousness becoming cold, detached, and mechanized, where thinking dominates the whole human being instead of serving an integrated spiritual life. The marvel character MODOK (depicted top left) visually embodies the Ahrimanic imbalance Steiner warns about: a hypertrophied head (abstract, calculating intellect) mechanically sustaining a diminished body, symbolizing intelligence as severed from the other activities of the ego/I. Grey aliens (depicted bottom) repeat the same. Enlarged heads signify hyper-intellect, while small hands and bodies indicate the atrophy of will and feeling under a one-sided dominance of cognition. Note: this is not a claim that the above figures are Ahrimanic (well, aside from Ahriman of course), it is only to point out the forms they take express an imaginary coherence which signify certain relations between intellect, other activities of the ego, and embodiment.

Multiplicity Band-Aid

Faced with the devastating inadequacy of the monolith IQ score, for example its inability to capture creativity, wisdom, social skills, and morality, the modern paradigm does not reconsider its foundations. Instead, it performs the classic move of a failing ideology. It multiplies its errors and reproduces them. This is done by stepping back from the “one” score, and stating that in reality, there are multiple “scores” for the multiple “intelligences”. The retreat from the single “g” factor, to the theory of “multiple intelligences” is certainly not progress, it is the desperate attempt to preserve the core reductionist project of conceiving the mind as a quantifiable collection of measurable traits by fragmenting the trait of “intelligence” into more pieces when the original “concept” proves too elusive. There is a fundamental category error at play here. The domain/medium in which intelligence “operates” is mistaken for a distinct type of intelligence. This is as ludicrous as saying there is a “hammering” intelligence, and a “sawing” intelligence, rather than the recognizing a source in both actions as a unitary mechanical understanding applied to different tools. The manifestation of a faculty is not a separate faculty, and so listing applications of intelligence and pretending that these are in fact a result of the theory of the applicator is empty posturing. Not entirely empty, since it perfectly mirrors the fragmented, dissociated, consciousness of the modern age at its endpoint. Having first shattered the living world into dead matter, and then shattered the living subject into a solitary intellect, it now shatters intellect itself into a checklist of intellects. Hyper-specialization of this kind is the bureaucratic mind’s last refuge in the face of the problem of quality. If you can’t measure the whole, then you subdivide it until you can measure the parts.

This is where we must re-assert the unitary character of intelligence, grounded in our initial exploration of the concept. Intelligence is not six or seven different things. It is the singular core capacity to discern. What varies is not the intelligence, but the field in which it reads (math, social scenarios, kinesthetic), the tools it uses (logic, ideally empathy, rhythm/awareness of the body), and the familiarity one has with the previous two. The musician who perceives harmonic tension, and the mathematician discerning a numeric contradiction are employing fundamentally the same faculty of intelligence. The difference lies in the pattern and the mediums. Furthermore, the fragmentation is not benign. It reproduces and diffuses the same normative implication that IQ, with the monolithic “g”, did. The single-value model created a blunt hierarchy of intelligence. “If there are higher and lower intelligences, then it necessarily follows that individuals with higher intelligences should be valued”, whatever that means. Operationally, it acted (and continues to act) as a perfect tautology for those who wish to take its result and vindicate this or that theory of man and intelligence. In assuming a taxonomy of general cognitive worth (“g”) and designing puzzles to isolate and measure it, it uses the resulting scores as proof that such a taxonomy is real, mistaking its operational definition for a discovery about nature. The multiple-intelligences model, in response, creates a sprawling map of ranked categories. All it does is expand this process. It creates a taxonomy of separate mental modules, then designs domain-specific tasks to measure them. After that, it uses these results to validate the initial compartmentalization. In both cases, the test’s designs predetermine its conclusion. The underlying impulse remains unchanged, this being the compulsion to sort, label, then compare. Whether claiming a group excels in “mathematical” intelligence or lacks in “spatial” intelligence, the framework still traffics in the implication of normativity. It provides a more nuanced, albeit culturally sensitive, lexicon for that same old project of cataloguing human worth by type. This is the more polite 21st century face of the much older logic which believes that human minds can be definitively taxonomized, and that these taxonomies justify social and educational destiny. Generalities certainly do exist, but only as the result of process. It is never a revelation of innate type. The observation that populations exposed to generations of colonialism, resource deprivation, and the deliberate destruction of their own cultural-linguistic frameworks might, and generally tend to do, perform differently on tests designed by and for another population group even within the same ethnicity (say for example an educated class versus a working class which does not have the same opportunity for education or resources), let alone between different cultures. At this point, the IQ test is a number which reflects the culture and access to resources one’s parents have. For example, to look at the lower average scores of Haitian and Somali populations and conclude something about “African intelligence” is to commit the gravest error of the Ahrimanic paradigm. The effect is confused for the cause. The cause is not a cognitive deficiency, the cause is the legacy of material dispossession, lack of education, and cultural disorientation. These generalities, far from vindicating a taxonomy of racial or ethnic intellectual worth, should be read as indictments of the very systems (colonial, economic, and now psychometric) that created the conditions for the disparity and then designed the instrument to measure its consequence as if they were its origins. To see the score and believe in something like this is to be fooled by the most insidious of self-fulfilling prophecies and out oneself as not having the capacity to make the IQ test intelligible to themselves. In other words, it is the sign of an unintelligent person.

The Future of Intelligence

Mont Sainte-Victoire series by Paul Cézanne
Cézanne here is painting the act of perception itself.

The sovereign “I”, or the Consciousness Soul as Steiner would call it, is left analyzing a disenchanted world, leading to alienation as well as this understanding of what constitutes “intelligence”. If the modern error is to substitute the abstract metric for the living faculty, then any path forward must return to the act itself. The solution to the IQ paradigm, this current understanding of intelligence, is certainly not to design more abstract tests. It lies not in a refinement of metrics or further retreat into positivism. The answer is to remove proxy altogether. We must stop trying to simulate intelligence in a vacuum and instead test it in situ by evaluating the performance of the action that constitutes it. This means shifting our focus from the disembodied skill of “pattern recognition” to the more concrete act of, for example, deciphering a historical text’s latent causes, or diagnosing a complex system. Instead of attempting to measure “abstract reasoning” through logic puzzles, the test of abstract thinking would be engaging in the works of Hegel, where intelligence, or intelligibility is demonstrated in the faithful following of thought in abstract thought. In such a framework, assessment almost becomes inseparable from education and practice. You’d never measure a surgeons’ intelligence with a paper quiz on anatomy (although I’d hope they know their anatomy). The assessment is done by observing it in the theater and viewing how the surgeon integrates knowledge and sensorimotor skills, with on-the-fly Judgment under pressure. Nor would you measure the capability of a basketball player in playing basketball with their squat and push-up limits. You would assess them by doing the activity of interacting with a ball. Intelligence as being the capacity to make things intelligible can only be authentically evaluated when it is engaged in the actual work of rendering a real context and content intelligible. The move from abstracted metric to enaction is the first step out of Ahriman’s cage of quantification, since it respects intelligence for what it is as a relational event. What the future of intelligence necessarily requires is to go beyond the paradigm of this kind of measurement altogether and into a comprehensive reintegration. Firstly, a healing of the subject-object split that defines modern consciousness is needed. The individuated “I” (subject), having fully won its sovereignty, must freely choose to reunite with the world (object) in partnership. The goal is to retain the modern ego’s clarity and analytical power, while shedding its alienation from the world and thereby regaining a participatory, living connection to the world. In the future, intelligence will no longer be a number on a sheet, it will be recognized once against as a quality of relationship manifest in action and creation. The future of intelligence advances to what Rudolf Steiner foresaw as the next stage of consciousness: the development of the spirit-self. In the future, the status of the conscious “I” transforms. Having fully secured its individuality and analytical power through the modern “consciousness soul”, it will now turn these capacities outward with a new intention. The “I” will not experience itself as a spectator locked inside a skull, observing a dead, mechanical, world outside its grasp. Through disciplined inner development, consciousness can and will evolve to perceive the living ideas and spiritual forces actively shaping the world. In this act of perception, there is a transcendence of the passive observation of sense-certainty. It begins with meticulous phenomenological attention to the object. Whether it be a plant or a social dynamic, it does not matter, what matters is not interpreting the thing in front of you as a dead thing, but as a dynamic appearance. The observer then actively engages in a process of participatory thinking, striving to trace the living idea, or spiritual form, manifesting through the phenomenon. One does not impose external theory here. Through a disciplined imagination (which has a specific definition for Steiner), one follows the logic of the object’s own becoming. The questions asked are not along the lines of “what causes it?”, they are asking along the lines of “What is its inherent necessity? What spiritual form reveals itself as this particular phenomenal form?”. In doing so, the thinker’s consciousness aligns with the generative principle of the object. The subject no longer stands in opposition to a foreign object. By thinking the object’s own formative reality from within, the act of perception becomes the very medium in which the object’s essentiality is realized. The subject-object split is healed in this activity. The subject’s perceiving is the object’s self-disclosure. Thus, a mental illness is not reduced to neural correlates, but understood as a coherent, albeit pathological, shape of meaning and living experience, whose necessity can be discerned in its own terms. True intelligence, now and in the next age, is the capacity for this kind of perception, where the mode of intelligibility consciousness takes is characterized by uniting with the intelligible form in the act of its apprehension, thereby finding the self back into the world, where it never really left.

Our long examination of intelligence has concluded with a reorientation of an old definition. We traced the phantom of our modern intelligence from its origins as an act of making things intelligible to us, to its imprisonment as a quantified score, a journey which mirrors consciousness’s own evolution into the alienated sovereign “I”. IQ, in addition to being a flawed metric, is the perfect character of this deeper spiritual crisis. It is an artifact of a consciousness that has disenchanted the world, and now, in its loneliness, can conceive of no higher purpose than assigning itself an empty label. However, as the crisis reveals a disease, it also reveals a cure. The future of intelligence calls us not to invent a new test or metric, but to undertake a new kind of literacy that renders ourselves intelligible to a universe that is, and always has been, profoundly intelligible. It invites us to step into dialogue with the world, where the most intelligent thing you can do is participate in a mutual revelation of meaning.

item.title: Time Travel is a Christian Prospect
item.description:
Intro

When “time travel” is mentioned, the snarky retort of “we are travelling through time” often follows. What is meant by this is that by the very concept of being in time means that we are subject to the flow of time; to the power of Kronos. To state this only is to state the obvious. Of course when a prisoner travels from his cell to the yard, no one calls him a “traveler”. Yet he travels. Mere subjection to time, that fact of endurance in nature, is not what one means when time travel is evoked. The kind of time travel that sparks our imagination, the kind that carries spiritual significance and weight, is a question about agency, the degree to which one can step outside the determinations of nature, move or rebel against it, or perceive what lies beyond its power. Insofar as time travel exists, it must be of this latter sort, closely occupied with the question of freedom and agency. The aim here is to demonstrate what time travel actually is and its possibility.

To get a better grasp of what is meant, we may look to examples in fiction. When popular culture imagines time travel, it tends to reach for a very mechanical sense of the process, say the flux capacitor in the DeLorean, or the “bigger on the inside” spatially of the Tardis. The assumption shared by these icons is that time is another spatial dimension one can navigate with the right machine, a mechanical solution to a mechanical setting. One simply points a machine in the right “temporal direction” and moves. The appeal here is the promise of change without really doing anything. You remain yourself, only relocated to 1955, or the dawn of the universe. However, this materialist fantasy fails in that it mistakes the mere function of displacement in time for freedom, assuming that agency over time is a matter of engineering instead of a metaphysical endeavor. The machine does not change who you are, only changing where and when you are. Similarly to the prisoner, he may walk to the guard’s desk, the yard, to his cell, or to the cafeteria, but he remains a prisoner.

A more clever exploration of temporal agency appears in H. G. Wells’ The Time Machine. In this story, the Time Traveler constructs a framework for asking what time travel means and implies for man. His machine is quite selective and purposeful, but the story’s point is concerned with what one finds at the journey’s end. The traveler arrives in the year 802,701 to discover a humanity bifurcated into the Eloi and the Morlocks, as a projection of Wells’ anxiety about class division and the implications of the industrial revolution. His agency in time refuses the cheap “theme park-ification” of the past, opting for the revelation that time itself carries consequences. What Wells captures, among other themes and truths, is a thing which the materialist obscures in the form of a red herring. Genuine agency as related to time is never about locomotion, but about relation, something more ideal. Relation to the past, the future, and eternal now, and to the structures of power that would either reduce us to unfree beings, or become conducive to our budding sense of freedom. To understand time travel, we must first clear up what time is. The materialist treats time as a kind of neutral container, an additional fourth dimension alongside the three of space, assuming that space and time are commensurable. In other words, that the same logic governing extension in space governs the indifferent succession of time. It does not. Space, in its indifferent differentiation to point, line, and plane, presents us with a manifold where every dimension can substitute for another without altering the whole. Time, by contrast, has a for-itself difference. Its moments are not interchangeable in the same way. This may help to explain the resulting paradoxes, such as the “grandfather paradox”, and contradictions which result in this kind of conception.

Perhaps a metaphor will be useful here. Imagine an individual walking the perimeter of a circle on the ground, and trying to find the cause of the circle in the motion of the ciruclar movement itself. The circle’s motion (here the walking), is the phenomena itself, rather than the origin. So displacement in time, as if “displacement” is comparable to spatial displacement, never really has a conclusion. Additionally, the walker themselves never escape the motion of the circle as the symptom of a mistaken premise; that is, the misunderstanding of temporality leads to an enclosed circularity, never to be escaped because it never ended and never began. In other words, grandfather paradoxes and other similar logical indeterminacies are circular movements in space, simply transposed a pseudo “dimension” (that is, time as a misconceived extension of spatiality) higher into time.

A deeper consideration reveals to us that time as we experience it with waking consciousness is not a true spiritual reality, it is a material one, and thus a perspectival image, a Maya which serves its purpose. Just as we instinctively transpose three-dimensional space onto a two-dimensional surface and learn to read perspective in space, so too must we learn to see the perspective in time, that is, to place every being at its correct point in time, recognizing that what appears causally sequentially in the present may, in truth, be the working of spiritual forces. Time itself, far from being a neutral dimension, originates in the collaboration of the spiritual hierarchies. It came into being through the sacrificial deed of higher spirits, giving birth to the spirits (or concept) of time known as Archai. To speak of time travel is not to speak of navigating a spatialized fourth dimension. It involves relating consciously to the spiritual substance of time itself, particularly to the Time-Spirits who construct and guide the epochs, and to the Father’s power which underlies all nature as its eternal ground.

What time is it?

If we are to speak of time travel, we must first dispense the notion mentioned that time is a neutral contained. Time is not space. The moments of time do not coexist. They negate one another. The past is not, yet it was, the future is not, yet it will be, and the present is the only vanishing point between the two. The essence of time is negativity posited for itself, the “negative unity of being outside of itself”. It is, in Hegel’s formulation, the Kronos who gives birth to everything and destroys his offspring. This is the Kronos of ancient myth; the devouring father and the power that generates only to consume, it is that inexorable force which subordinates everything finite to its own transience. Kronos is the abstract negativity that underlies all temporal existence. What spiritual science adds to this picture is the recognition that this abstract negativity (rather, time as we experience it in waking consciousness) is not a given. It was created for a purpose. Time came into being through the work of spiritual beings who structure and order the conditions of succession and casualty. In effect, time is for spirit.

The Kronos of the philosopher successfully achieves the appearance of time within the sphere of nature. It is really the power of the Father, insofar as the Father is encountered as abstract law. The power that determines figurations as regular and establishes the relationships that science seeks to know is the work of the Father. But this is the Father in His exterior aspect. That power that holds nature in its lawful order, the Kronos who binds all things to the cycle of generation and decay. Yet time itself is not merely this devouring power. The concept of time is eternal. As Hegel himself says, “for it is neither just any time, nor the moment now, but time as time is its concept”, and this concept, in its self-identity is absolute negativity and freedom. Spiritual science gives the insight which Absolute Idealism provides a concrete spiritual content. The Archai, that is the Time-Spirits, are the true ancient time as the children of the Cherubim and Thrones. Time is the activity of these beings. The abstract Kronos which appears to us as the relentless flow of moments is the externalized image of the spiritual process constituted by the sacrificial giving and receiving which constitutes the cosmic order.

What this means for our inquiry is that time is truthfully a medium of relationship between the hierarchies, particularly between the Father and creation, and ultimately between the human soul and the eternal. Divisions of past, present, and future are certainly real, in a perspectival, relative sense (not to be confused with the “relativity” of Einstein, which no son or daughter of mine will support). They are forms through which we, in our state of separation from the spiritual world, experience the operations of beings who themselves dwell in a more unified relationship to time (or the time-spirits).

The question of time travel asks of man through spiritual means to relate to time as the time-spirits do. Can one learn to place every being at its correct point in time (in effect, thinking as the Time-Spirits do), while recognizing that on a higher register, linear succession is an assistant? To do this, we must then venture forth from what time is to its higher ideality in how it actually works as a field of spiritual agency.

Father time and the spirits

The reign of Kronos, the Titan of linear implacable time, is our subjection to the generation and decay of nature. Much can be said on the significance of Zeus slaying him and taking up the filled slot of Father. On the other hand, we have the qualitative reality of time as the workings of spiritual beings. They construct the epochs and hold open the possibility of development, redemption, history, and so on. These two powers are not identical, but are closely related, for both find their ultimate ground in the Father, whose nature is the substance of time itself. Everything in nature is subject to time, everything in time is subject to the Father. It’s almost a trope how mystics and those with experiential knowledge of spiritual worlds speak of the invalidity of spatio-temporality as we know it. For example, in the astral world, two beings can occupy the same space simultaneously. More has been said on this in another article.

In any case, the mythological imagination of Kronos is the father who devours his own children. This image conveys exactly the philosophical concept earlier- the pure abstraction of becoming that gives birth to everything, only to consume it once again. In this aspect, time is the manifestation of the Father’s power in its most external form. This is the Father encountered as gravity, entropy, causality, force, and such. In the natural order’s indifference to individual destiny, this relentless submission to sequence is the Father’s work. However, this is the Father as a kind of veiled figure, an abstract universal. Kronos knows no mercy, because he knows only the necessity of his own law. Modern science worships this power unknowingly. To be subject to this power implies an almost slave-like disposition, and likewise, the materialist conception of time travel, with its machine on machine manipulation, remains wholly within this dominion. It is a rebellion against the power of Kronos, while at the same time remaining subject to his ultimate sovereignty. Recall the prisoner metaphor; the materialist conception of time travel is almost like the prisoner mistaking the movement towards the yard for true freedom, that is, the freedom from something. It is doomed to be held apart from self-determination, or true freedom.

The Archai represent the internal spiritual reality of time, enlightening us on its origin and purpose. In their domain, in the domain of spiritual realms, the past is present as an influence, and the future is the “now” which occupies the present as the determinations spirit knows. They are beings who allow for spiritual development, for the possibility that what has been bound by Kronos can be loosened. They are the powers through whom history becomes the mark of the procedural development for freedom. This is all to say that the purpose of time, while being seen as restrictive conditioning, is in all ways necessary for the development of freedom.

Neither Kronos nor the Archai are ultimate. Freedom is ultimate. The “Father” (of which a distinction is to be made between Kronos, who is a father-figure, and the Father of the Christian Trinity) is the very subsistence of nature itself, echoing the Christian intuition that ““In Him we live and move and have our being” (Acts 17:28). It would be mistaken to view the Father as some distant legislator, setting the laws of nature in motion and then withdrawing, never to be seen again, as seen with the God of Deism. Space and time are emanations of His single, indivisible being, as one component (do not attack me for the usage here, how else should one refer to aspects of the Trinity?) of salvation. Our understanding of time is then transformed as a medium by which the Father speaks creation into being and sustains it. That is why Steiner says that “everywhere in nature is the Father”; He speaks to his creation through the mundane fact of being. To characterize Kronos as the Father misses the fact that He is not exhausted by law, He also comprises that medium of relation designated as the covenant between creator and created.

The possibility of “time travel”, and it’s meaning explored

So how can one “travel through time”? Even the phrase itself is extremely loaded. It suggests the kind of movement we have already done away with: a subject moving through a medium, as if it were a road, and we are just there along for the ride. If time isn’t this “container” idea explained before, but the activity of spiritual beings which create an environment conducive to freedom, and if its substance is not recognized as slavery, but an aspect of the Father’s reality He has given to us, then what we call “time travel” might be better described as “time relationship”. This would involve entering into relation with the powers that constitute time, and knowing, but not necessarily accepting, the forces which seek to bend temporal flow toward their own ends.

We have already spoken for the Archai, or the Time-Spirits, who do not move through time, but create it. Their relation to time is one of agency, specifically overseeing epochs and enabling the development of human freedom. Ahrimanic and Luciferic beings, like all spirits, do not dwell in the present as we do. Ahriman works from the past. His influence streams into the present from earlier ages, fixing things into rigid law and mechanism, promoting the illusion that what is determined cannot be changed. Lucifer, on the other hand, works from the future. He seduces one with possibilities unmoored from reality, from the now, pulling the soul toward premature spiritual heights that bypass process. Both seek to divert and distort the proper rhythm of time. Ahriman would freeze it into eternal repetition, Lucifer would dissolve it into formlessness. Neither has reverence for the Father, nor recognize it as necessary for the true development of man.

This all stands in stark contrast to modern fantasies of time travel. One of the more bizarre offspring of materialist thinking is “Roko’s Basilisk”, a thought experiment in which a future superintelligence punishes those who did not help bring it about, retroactively creating a motivation that reaches back through time. This is a parody of spiritual agency through an attempt to coerce the past by fear, reducing time to a deterministic chain that can be manipulated for a hypothetical future. What the Basilisk reveals, without knowing, is that the materialist framework necessarily must treat time as a thing to be exploited, since it has no place for love or freedom, the kind which binds moment to moment together. The Basilisk is in effect Kronos without the power of the Father, a purely devouring abstraction which only generates itself, much like the function of cancer.

We may also consider the human soul between death and a new birth. After death, the soul undergoes a reverse journey through the spheres, gradually shedding the earthly body and its attachments, memories, and such. Among the sharper minds reading this, a contradiction might present itself: if the soul is higher than the body, how then would it re-experience life? Would this not be like trying to fit an ocean into a single drop of water? The answer is quite simple. The higher “I” does not experience the sentient soul, or the part of the intellectual soul concerned with external sensory perception, only the members of the soul concerned with sense-free thinking, morality/moral ideas, art, etc., just as when the clairvoyant “thinks” as the elemental does (or perceives the spiritual being such that concept and percept are unified), they do not become the elemental. Similarly, the Archangel does not “see” these same aspects of the soul when looking at the human. However, the soul still experiences time in its perspectival reality. It looks back on the life just lived as a spatial panorama, and it prepares for a future incarnation by working with higher beings to shape the conditions of its next earthly life in accordance with it’s karma. Does man ever “reincarnate in the past”? Not in the sense of living an earthly life earlier than its previous one. Incarnation follows a forward succession because the Earth’s own evolution has direction. But the soul between death and rebirth moves in a realm where past and future are not as they appear to us as linear opposites. It participates in a domain where the rules of spatio-temporality do not apply, where it is reversible in influence, and oriented toward the redemption of what has been. The fact that reincarnation itself wasn’t always a thing should provide hints relating to the procedural affectations the soul undergoes.

It should also come as no surprise that certain forms of human perception can, under specific conditions, bypass the apparent constraints of spatiotemporality. Phenomena grouped under the names ESP and clairvoyance, such as remote viewing (which is a very sterilized form of clairvoyance for the purposes of military surveillance) and oneiromancy or prophetic dreams, all share a common feature. Space and time ceases to be a barrier. A remote viewer may describe a location thousands of miles away as if present, or an event from a distant century with the same immediacy as the morning’s breakfast. In these states, time does not matter since the perceiving soul has, for a moment, loosened its identification with the physical body and entered a mode of consciousness nearer to that of the astral world. One lives in time as much as one lives in space during this. A brief sidenote: the definition of death is the separation of the soul from the body, causing degeneration. While complete death is not possible, it would be irresponsible to suggest one engage in such activities. In fact, it may be the case that what is more conducive towards freedom involves sacrificing this capability in the name of God. Such activities are natural extensions of the perspectival nature of time. Importantly, these capacities do not grant mastery over Kronos. They are brief, fragmentary glimpses of what lies behind his immediate appearance to us.

Christian time travel

So far, even when we recognize the relationality of time, and that human souls already participate in temporal movements beyond the linear, we confront a limit. No being, whether human, animal, or “angelic”, can simply align itself with the Father’s perspective by its own power. Kronos holds us in his grip. The Archai, for all their wisdom, are not the Father, they serve His order but do not bridge the gap between His infinite perspective and our finite, temporal condition. In order to be drawn into the Father’s self-relation to time, that is, to stand outside of degeneration without ceasing to be the kind of being we are, another figure is required in the picture. We require a mediator who is both fully of the Father, and fully within time, one whose life, death, and resurrection open a way through the binding law of nature.

The eternal Word, the selfsame “through whom all things were made”, did not look upon His creation from the impassable summit of eternity. He entered it, taking flesh from birth, to hunger, to the slow accumulation of the years, to the stinging of crucifixion, and at last to the cold threshold of death. In this act, the Father’s perspective, which appears to us as Kronos’s grip, itself descends into the very sequence it ordained. Kronos, who seemed nothing but degeneration, came across Christ and his act of redemption. Christ was the one who took the past, including the sins and failures of humanity, into His own body on the cross and transformed it. The crucifixion became the axis, the fixed point by which the wheel of time turns. He is the Alpha and the Omega, the beginning and the end. In Him, the linear succession of moments is gathered into a single, presence.

When Christ says, “Do this in remembrance of me” (Luke 22:19), He is in effect instituting collapsing the conception of time which makes all things fade. We may take the Greek anamnesis as a word describing making something present. For instance, in the Eucharist the sacrifice of Calvary is represented as opposed to repeated. The offering becomes accessible to all, no matter age or congregation. This is the truest sense of “time travel”, a relationship with time involving spiritual participation in the eternal act, beyond the confines of spatiotemporality and the contingencies of nature. The Eucharist also reveals the relational nature of time as a feast, looking backwards to the cross, forward to the coming kingdom, and inward to the unity of the body of Christ. In the sacred meal, the faithful are lifted out of the degenerating current of Kronos and placed within the eternal time of the Father, mediated through the son.

The Archai, or Time-Spirits, oversee epochs and hold time open for the encroaching development of human freedom. Yet even they away fulfillment. Freedom itself requires a concrete source, and that source is Christ. In Him, the human “I” is united with the divine “I Am”. The Archai can be said to guide humanity through cultural ages. If this is the case, which it is, then Christ is the goal toward which those ages move. Hence, the Christian relationship to time participates in time by rightfully recognizing what time is. The believer, united to Christ shares in His resurrection, sitting with Him in heavenly places. From that vantage, the divisions of past, present, and future truly become relative. The Christian can pray for the dead with the assurance that in Christ, the boundary is permeable. He can intercede for the future with the confidence that his prayer shapes what is to come, and he can repent of the past with the certainty that all is forgiven and transformed.

“Time travel” is finally unveiled to us as the interaction with time that finds us becoming the kind of being who can dwell in the Father’s time. What has been explained thus far is the promise of theosis. Through union with Christ, the human person who comes to share in the divine mode of existence. To “travel through time” is to be drawn into the eternal movement of love between Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit, a “movement” that generates time, and is not bound by what it makes. It is to stand with Christ at the wedding at Cana, and at the foot of the cross, and at the empty tomb, and at the feast of the Lamb, all in one eternal now, all in one indivisibility. This is the substance of faith, and the destiny of those who are in Christ. And it is offered freely, to all who will receive it, and to all who, receiving, cease to become arrested by time. This is the true meaning of “time travel”.

item.title: Towards an Esoteric Christian Weltanschuung: On Comprehension and Forgiveness
item.description:
The Advent of Thought

The emergence of thought in man has brought with it a cornucopia of reverberations, the fruits of which are laid bare in the various works, paintings, books, sculptures, cultures, and beliefs that he himself holds. Thought did not always exist in man. However, now that it does, it is in the best interest of the thinking man to detail and reflect upon what exactly it gives him, the ground upon which it can develop in him, and, consequently, what it says about him such that he can recognize it as his own self-expression. As Laozi bids in the Dao De Ching,

“He who knows others is wise; he who knows himself is enlightened.”

To begin with, a definition is of service, that is, one that helps illuminate what the action most relevant to discussion is. The word which we will define presents itself to us as thought. Thought is the product of thinking. The word think emerged initially as a “a convergence of two Old English verbs from the same prehistoric source but with distinct forms and senses”. The first, þyncan, meant “to seem” or “to appear”, preserved in the archaic methinks. The second, þencan, meant “to conceive in the mind, meditate, or intend”, and is the verb that gives us our modern sense of cognitive reasoning. Grammatically, the latter was actually the causative for the former, implying an original sense of “to cause (something) to appear to oneself”. During the Middle English period, these two verbs merged in the form of “thinken”, finding the older sense of the word as “seem” largely absorbed and/or lost. The connection to the noun “thought” is fairly direct. Thought derives from the Old English þoht or geþoht, which was the past participle of þencan, thus, a “thought” is proven through etymological reconstruction, in addition to colloquial understanding, as the product of the act of thinking. Both “think” and “thought”, along with the related word “thank”, ultimately trace back to the same PIE (Proto-Indo-European) tong-, meaning “to think or feel”, uniting these concepts of mental activity, its results, and its remembrance into a single family.

Of importance here are two things: remembrance and feeling. Remembrance, or memory, is necessary in order for this act to retain structural continuity with itself. For an act of thinking to be more than a passing, fleeting impulse, it must reference and build upon previous mental content. Memory is precisely the faculty that allows a thought to cohere and develop from a semblance (þyncan), into a concept (þencan, “to cause to seem to oneself”). It allows perceptions and reflections on those percepts to combine into a continuous process. Without it, each cognitive act would be alienated from its origin, incapable of forming the sustained patterns we recognize as reasoning and contemplation. Feeling, likewise, can be considered a qualitative motive. Modern sensibilities find this a bit confused, holding “thought” and “feeling” as antinomies. The thinking person is considered to be one that produces from themselves the logical results of their thought, undiluted with illogical feeling. The emotional person is regarded, or rather disregarded, as ungrounded and swayed by the intense immediacies of emotion, to be corrected by a composed and reserved logic. This kind of parody of the relationship between thought and feeling misses the interplay of the two as they rightfully are. It is often people that are driven by intense emotion which employ logic and thought how they do, seeing them as reasonable centers by which emotions peripherally orbit and are attracted by. In “opposition”, one may feel very emotional towards that which they deem logical and true. Both perspectives are validated if we are to be called free beings, but what is meant to be emphasized is that the root word’s association of thought with feeling indicates to us that early conceptions of thinking were charged with valuation and personal resonance. To think about something was to have it matter to you on some level. To desire it (as in Old English’s þencan’s sense of “wish, desire”), and to appraise its appropriateness was to recognize the unity of thought with this thought, or my thought. Feeling is what transforms a neutral mental image into an object of our intention and concern. It becomes the bridge between the apparition of seeming, and the meaningful cognition found in thinking.

Having defined thinking as this internally generative act of causing something to appear to oneself, we can say that this product called “thought” is a thing which describes an active process that we wholly generate and inhabit. Defined as internal act, we may extrapolate this into the understanding that when we have a thought, we are within every fiber of this thought, a complete participator in its activity. Unlike observing a rose, where its hidden qualities may elude us, a thought contains nothing that you have not yourself thought into it. Thought is the one element of our consciousness of which we have the most immediately complete possession of. It is the most intimate of knowledge kinds, because we are the originators of this knowledge. In other words, the feeling of being fully active and present within thought is a proof of its internal reality and origin.

Every time we think, we cannot just be describing a thing we see (so for example, a single triangle, or a specific lion). We are internally re-constructing it in relation to its own ideal possibility- to its concept. To think of a “tree” is to hold in mind not just a pine or an oak, but the generative principle of “tree-ness” itself, including its form, growth, and life. What we gesture towards, in this internal act of participation (or self-particularizing), is that we recognize the instance by connecting it to the universal pattern it expresses in the form of its concept. The concept is what holds a given perception together as a unity, which retroactively makes percepts intelligible in the first place. Moreover, this internality exists everywhere. If the concept of a tree is the unitary pattern by which all trees exist in virtue of, then that given pattern is not a human invention. We discover it through thinking. The cosmos is structured by these living patterns, or concepts (the various laws of form, growth, etc.), and our thinking is the site where these concepts become conscious of themselves. We are the point where the universe turns back in on itself to understand itself. In this sense, the saying that “we are the ‘thoughts’ of God”, coheres with common understanding. It is in effect saying that we are, the self-directed and conscious expressions of that cosmic intelligence, and at the same time saying that we are downstream and conditioned by some absolute totality. Our very capacity to think conceptually is our share in the expressions of this world-shaping activity.

A distinction can then be made between the local act of thought and the global. Or, in more spiritual terms, “human” thought and “cosmic” thought. On the one hand, we have the global delineation of thought, this universal process of internal generation, present in all things, and on the other, the instantiation of this act in us as individual people. This raises a point for us. If thinking is our primary tool for engaging with reality, then the specific way we orient our thinking will determine the reality we perceive. The activity of human thought decides what counts as the world for us. Different starting points in thought concretize into attitudes. They develop logically from basic mental postures. Once thought awakens in man as his primary faculty, thought begins to explore its relationship to being. Each differentiated attitude becomes itself a rudimentary status of thought which positions itself as answering the question of what one recognizes as ultimately significant for the soul. These positions are hereby known as worldviews: conceptions of the “world”, or object, in the soul of man, as provided by thinking.

Zodiacal Associations

Spirit has been roughly defined before here, and so laboring to explain it in detail is re-treading ground, wasting time. However, what has not been defined before is the zodiac. Ordinary knowledge recognizes the zodiac as the belt of space in the sky, which is divided into 12 sections. The Sun then passes through each section. Astrology, as it goes with the materialistic sciences, has extracted all ideal significance from this “map”, reducing it into a way to tell the wobble of the Earth, or a way to tell time, resembling an archaic form of the independent variable found in the laboratory- time. The thinking man would best be fit for exploring all aspects of the concept of the zodiac. It is, in actuality, a topography of man’s nature and spiritual potential. The constellations correspond to the highest spiritual beings, such as the Seraphim and Cherubim, who sacrificed their substance to create our solar system. One can consider it a structural necessity that if there is life, there were higher beings which made it a condition that they influence and, in some cases, intervene on the development of this life. Likewise, any given “solar system” (which we may define as an appropriately partitioned section of the cosmos) hosting life is considered an organism, and will develop a zodiac. Surely, this stands in contrast to the tabloid conceptions of the zodiac as influencing personality traits. The signs actually act as cosmic formative forces in the development of man. For example, the emergence of the Heart in the form of man was founded under Leo (the Lion), speech under Taurus (the Bull), and symmetry under Gemini (the Twins). In the sense that higher beings act as influences on our development, the zodiac is both a map in the sky, and a map in ourselves. It is a picture of the spiritual archetype of man, projected into space, including man’s origin and future potential.

Building from the PIE root gwei-, meaning to live, the zodiac unveils itself as the animating intelligence of the cosmos. The Latin spiritus (breath, spirit) can help us in cognizing and recognizing the cosmos as a state of breath; the universe is always in a cycle of inspiring and expiring. The zodiac stands as a pattern of this breath. Each of the twelve signs represents a mode of this universal life-force in the form of a specific way in which the universe performs its living, or animating, or breathing. Aries (the Ram), for example, is the primal impulse to be, the fiery spark (the element of fire does not always refer to the state of matter ‘plasma’, but can also be considered the spiritual force of passion). Taurus is the power of persistence and embodiment. Gemini is the capacity to relate and communicate. These patterns continue throughout all twelve signs, describing a completed circle of animation.

Human thoughts are, accordingly, the localized incarnations of these animating forces. We, and hence our thoughts, are microcosms borne of this living cosmos, and our capacity to think shares in this principle. When we think, we are actually engaging in specific modes of cosmic living, transforming them into conscious activity. Thought dedicated to meticulous analysis and refinement is Virgo at work. The quality of thought which concedes ideality and spirituality yet prioritizes reconciliation and balance is the work of Libra. The drive to seek philosophical truth is Sagittarius animating our curiosity, while the need to build enduring conceptual structures is Capricorn’s formative will thinking through us, and so on and so forth. We will get to the zodiacal associations soon enough. Each of the twelve attitudes, or worldviews given to us by thought, can be understood as perspectives birthed when the mind operates predominantly under the influence of one, or more, of these twelve animating principles. By virtue of thought, our capacity to have this internal world, is itself animated by, and an expression of, a particular zodiacal quality of the cosmos’ living intelligence. The twelve worldviews soon to be discussed are thus twelve characteristic ways the one animated reality reflects upon and comprehends itself through the human soul. Our thinking stances chart a path already paved by the influences of what is possible through the zodiac, and, like the Sun, we all must eventually pass through each 12 of the influences.

The Truth of Each Worldview

No single worldview adequately possesses the truth in any exclusive sense. Each is a one-sided, but necessary, perspective that the human spirit adopts as it evolves in consciousness. The spiritual scientist should be able to freely inhabit any of the worldviews and their configurations. What is meant to be centrally conveyed is that the truth is the whole. Thought, in crafting worlds internal to the soul, crafts a diversity of worlds, each with their own life. From a spiritual standpoint, these worldviews are not ranked by any kind of superiority. For those familiar with Hegel’s Science of Logic, a comparison can be made where Essence is not “more true” than Being (although Being “finds its truth” in Essence, this is not a ranking of worth). It would make no sense to so say this, nor would it make sense to create a ranking of “souls” and soul-outlooks ranging in degree of value. They are different soul-configurations of modes of perception, and are all equally valid as expressions of the human spirit engaging with the cosmos through the activity of thought. God would value each as a unique individuality all the same. As such, the developmental sequence of each from another shows a progression in human self-awareness. We will revisit this nascent wisdom later on.

Opposing worldviews exist in a dialectical, but fruitful, tension. One cannot be “more true” than the other. Each contains the seed of its opposite, and finds its completion only when the soul inwardly experiences the other. We can take materialism for example. Materialism posits that only the physical, measurable world is real. Its truth is the stubborn reality of the external, material world. The one-sidedness present in it is one of a denial of interiority and spirit. Idealism, on the other hand, posits that ideas and spirit, or consciousness, are primary. Its truth is the reality of the internal. The one-sidedness present in it is its potential disregard for concrete being. Although these are not diametrically opposed claims, they still inhabit opposites “sides” insofar as their claims on reality are exclusive. The point is that one must fully inhabit the materialist perspective until one confronts its own limits, e.g., the mystery of consciousness, the origin of natural laws, etc. At this limit, the truth of an “Idealism” emerges immanently from Materialism’s own questioning. The limit represents the birthplace of the opposing view. The opposite journey is equally valid. By holding both, you no longer have a worldview; you behold the spiritual reality that gave rise to both perspectives. In dealing with these worldviews, to rectify a one-sided perspective is to draw out the other from the self’s inner core. In the case of the materialist, the remedy is in drawing Spirit out from materialist explanations.

The 12 Worldviews

To restate for clarity, a worldview is the content of the soul’s perspective on the world and the self. Each worldview describes an engagement with the world, as a possible object of thought. As an example, the materialist and spiritualist are arguing about the core of the object itself, presenting each as the crux of how one should view the world. Fortunately, worldview is the layer of philosophic doctrine that can be debated logically. This is because it is the most consciously articulated layer by way of thought. Below are the 12 worldviews, listed in association with their zodiacal signs.

Materialism and Spiritualism

The most concrete and abundant worldview, and the one we will begin with (for no other reason than this being the most easily accessible and most relevant for our current time), is materialism. Its main tenant announces that only “physical matter” is fundamentally real. Everything in existence, be it consciousness or thoughts, is ultimately an epiphenomenon of complex material interactions; everything “non-material”, which historically has been a definition disputed, is simply an emergent phenomenon from the “lower” world of matter. The worldview certainly has validity within the domain of sense-perceptible phenomena, acting as the chief of understanding the laws of physics and chemistry and the like. However, when elevated to a total worldview, a problem occurs. To demonstrate this problem, Rudolf Steiner presents us two scenarios involving shoemakers. Imagine if the historical mystic Jakob Böhme, who was actually a shoemaker in Görlitz, had used the concepts and techniques of his trade to explain the cosmos. He might say,

“There is a great last at the foundation of the world. Over this last the world-leather was stretched; then the world-nails were added… The difference between day and night comes from the world-boot being polished in the morning and getting dirty by evening."

Everyone would rightfully laugh at this absurdity because shoemaking concepts have no validity for explaining cosmic phenomena. The real Jakob Böhme, however, knew better. He used his skilled knowledge of leather and soles to make excellent boots for the townspeople. But when he sought to understand the spiritual foundation of the world, he resorted to something else entirely. He resorted to inner contemplation and spiritual insight- concepts appropriate to that domain. He did not commit a kind of category error. Likewise, the error which materialism makes is in failing to see the concepts of matter and chemistry as tools for understanding the physical aspect of the world, not the world in its totality. The strict materialist worldview renders the human “I”, freedom, consciousness, spirit, and all other higher ideals as illusions, which is both philosophically bankrupt, and spiritually deadening. Ultimately, the materialist worldview looks outward and downward, seeking to explain the “higher” by way of the “lower”. Its strength lies in its commitment to empirical observation and logical consistency within the physical realm, however, its weakness can only be solved with the inclusion of Spiritualism.

Spiritualism (called Spiritism in some works, not to be confused with the movement of Spiritism asserts that spirit is the foundational reality. The physical, or material world is either an emanation, manifestation, or illusion, depending on the soul-mood, created by spirit. Consciousness is primary; the divine mind takes precedent and matter is secondary and derivative. Spiritualism acts as a counter polarity to materialism. It correctly identifies that the essence of being, and the forces of life and thought, cannot be derived from dead matter. It is valid for the domain of inner experience, morality, and artistic creation. However, in its one-sided form, it risks becoming dismissive of the tangible reality of the physical world, viewing it as a great Maya (which, is a stance closer to Pneumatism, but is still delineated from it). The Spiritualist looks inward and upward, seeking to explain “lower” matter, by the “higher” spirit.

From materialism, one may begin with a naïve materialism. Let us take, for example, the committed materialist neuroscientist. He maps the brain’s wetware, convinced that consciousness is a chemical illusion, cast by the synapses. To explain mind, he must reduce it to diagrams of neutral pathways and neurotransmitters, yet, in his work, lies the seed of his doubt. His instruments measure the brain’s activity, but the observer of those measurements remains outside the diagram. The very act of doing science presupposes a unified site (the “I”) that matter-based models cannot locate, pushing them to consider that perhaps we only deal with metal representations, the phenomena of perception (phenomenalism). This world of phenomena, however, is quite stubborn and operates with a similar consistency. It demands an explanation beyond subjective appearance, leading them to affirm a structured and objective reality (realism). Investigating this reality, he finds that it is not a collection of fixed particles as initially thought, but a field of forces and processes which the observable world is only a result of (dynamism). These forces appear to organize themselves into discrete, self-organizing systems, each a kind of sovereign center, or Monad (monadism). Finally, they confront the core question: what is the nature of a unifying, self-aware center? Surely it cannot be a composite material thing. The logic of the materialist’s journey here forces a singular conclusion. The ultimate reality of these centers, must be non-material Spirit (spiritism). A more practical path can be found in the extreme modern materialism of today. The materialism of a 19th century physiologist, for example, can, through its own rigor, unintentionally and directly demonstrate the necessity of spirit! By describing the human being as a perfect machine, it raises the question: “What intelligence designed this machine?”. Thus, the dead end of one-sided materialism can become the start of a spiritual quest.

Conversely, the transition from spiritualism to materialism often begins with a spiritualism that cannot adequately account for the brute fact and suffering of the physical world. Seeking firmer ground, it may move through Idealism and Rationalism, eventually hardening into a Mathematism that sees only abstract structure, which can then be interpreted materially. The transition from one side of a worldview to another, does not necessarily have to follow a linear, zodiacal path, however, I will attempt to outline a logical path by which the spiritual scientist may guide another outside the dogmatism of a given worldview.

The sign of the worldview of materialism is Cancer, the crab, corresponding to the human breastplate. It was under the sign that the human form developed the basics of the ribs and thoracic cage, as a structure which encases and protects the vital organs. The ribcage can be considered the container of the heart and lungs, organs which facilitate vitality and breath. The crab’s hard shell is the perfect natural symbol for this worldview in the sense that it represents limit and protection. The shell creates a definitive, physical boundary between the soft inner life and the outer world. Similarly, materialism says that the hard “factual” boundary of physical matter defines all reality, both protecting and denying non-physical explanations. The very actions of the crab in retreating in its shell of hard matter reflect the tendency of materialism to retreat into the base level of measurable, mechani-chemical processes. Spiritualism’s constellation is Capricorn (the sea-goat), whose human correspondence is the skeletal framework. The skeleton provides the internal structure which allows the body to stand and move. Spiritualism does just this, providing the enduring structural idea that spirit is the foundational reality. The sea-goat exists in both water and on land, which respectively are the material and spiritual, or temporary and enduring realms. It is a composite creature with the tail of a fish, and the forward section of a mountain goat. This signifies the spiritualist view that no matter the domain (on water, or on land), spirit permeates and overarches all levels of being. Cancer calls on us to ground the world in the tangible and immediate world, while Capricorn nudges us towards the enduring structure behind the immediate. In the human body, this is echoed by the ribs and the skeleton. One provides a protected space for life, and the other provides the framework that allows that life to stand tall and go beyond it’s limit. A complete human being requires both.

Sensualism and Pneumatism

The immediacy of experience finds sensualism granting validity solely to sense impressions and direct experience. Everything from the understanding and reason is peeled off, and likewise, only the raw impression is left. Sensualism (or sensationalism) proclaims all genuine knowledge as originating in, and validated by, sense perception. It yells out “I only believe what I can see, hear, touch, taste, or smell”. Anything untraceable to sensory data is considered speculation or fantasy; it is meaningless to metaphysics and comprehension. Logically, it is the result of a naïve materialism, one that is more sophisticated, since it acknowledges that we don’t experience “matter” directly, but rather sense data. The domain of validity is, like materialism, found in the empirical sciences, natural history, or really any discipline which relies on careful observation. It provides the ground of factual certainty about the external world. The sensualist, or sensationalist, can be described with an attitude of outward receptivity. The soul here is akin to a passive mirror, faithfully recording impressions upon it from the external world. The limit lies in the fact that it cannot account for the organizing principles of the mind, such as the unity of consciousness, which shapes and interprets these sensations but, are not themselves sensory. The strict sensualist struggles to explain how we move from raw sensation to coherent principles.

Pneumatism, on the other hand, sees the immediacy of experience and claims that the senses are a doorway to the spiritual breath, or pneuma, that animates and organizes the world. For the pneumatist, the growth of the plant cannot be accurately characterized with a sequence of observable stages. The plant is expressing an inner life force, just as a thought is not merely a brain, but the resulting activity of a spiritual being. The relational attitude here is in and through, seeking a reality manifesting within the immediately sensory world. While sensualism’s starkness is corrected with a re-enchantment, a one-sided pneumatism risks devaluing the disciplined observation of the sensory facts themselves. Its proper domain can be considered any family member of spiritual perception.

In the case of a sensualist emerging from their shell of immediacy, it would likely begin with a routine and steadfast observation of the sensory world. We can take a botanist, who happens to be a pure sensualist, kneeling in a meadow. Her truth is measured in data. The precise hue of a petal, or the exact scent compound presents the world as a magnificent, but closed, encyclopedia of sensory facts. She believes that to know a flower is to exhaustively provide a catalog of its empirical profile. After years of this, a revolution begins to stir in her heart.
